elements.text1: Akwai ra'ayoyi daban-daban akan Allah da kuma yaya Allah yake. Samun ganewar yaya Allah yake a zahirance. To dole kuma mu duba me Littafi Mai Tsarki ya faɗa yarda Allah ya bayyana kansa a gare mu acikin Littafinsa Mai Tsarki
elements.text2: Hol no Alla wayi tigi rigi? Yimɓe ina njogii miijooji kala — won heen ina cikka ko o woɗɗuɗo, mo anndaaka, won heen ko doole moƴƴe, walla laamɗo kosmos. Kono en ngalaa haaju e teskaade. Alla hollitii en hoore mum e nder deftere nde, ko ɗoon puɗɗortoɗen.
elements.text3: What is God really like? People have all sorts of ideas — some think of Him as distant and unknowable, others as a kind force, or a cosmic rule-giver. But we don’t have to guess. God has revealed Himself to us in the Bible, and that is where we begin.

elements.text1: Wanene Allah
elements.text3: Who is God?

elements.passageText1: <sup>3</sup>A kullum muna gode wa Allah, Uban Ubangijinmu Yesu Almasihu, duk sa'ad da muke yi muku addu'a,
elements.passageText3: <sup>3</sup>Kullum muna gode wa Allah, Uban Ubangijinmu Yesu Kiristi, sa’ad da muke addu’a dominku.
elements.passageText4: <sup>3</sup>3 Koo ndeye to e min ngaɗana'on do'aare e min ngetta Allah Nyaako Joomiraawo meeɗen Yeesu Almasiihu. E min ngettamo ngam mooɗon,
elements.passageText5: <sup>3</sup>We always give thanks to God, the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, when we pray for you,

elements.question1: Me wannan ayar ta gaya mana game da wanene Allah?
elements.question2: Hol ko oo aaye haalanta en hol ko woni Alla?
elements.question3: What does this verse tell us about who God is?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: This verse identifies God as ’the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ’, revealing that God is not an impersonal force but a personal being who stands in a Father-Son relationship with Jesus. It also shows that God is someone to whom we can give thanks and speak to in prayer.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses a very specific title for God here — what does that title tell you about the kind of relationship God has with Jesus, and what might that imply about His nature?

elements.text1: Wacce shaida ce akwai ta tabbatar da cewa akwai Allah
elements.text2: Hol ko holliti wonde Alla ina woodi?
elements.text3: What evidence is there that God exists?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>Dubi yadda sararin sama yake bayyana ɗaukakar Allah!
elements.passageText3: <sup>1</sup>Sammai suna shelar ɗaukakar Allah;
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>The heavens declare the glory of God; the sky displays his handiwork.

elements.question1: Me wannan ayar ta nuna game da duniyar halitta a matsayin shaida ga Allah?
elements.question2: Hol ko ndee aaye hollirta e aduna tagooje ko seede Alla?
elements.question3: What does this verse suggest about the natural world as evidence for God?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The verse suggests that creation itself is a witness to God’s existence and glory. The sky and heavens are not neutral — they actively ’declare’ and ’display’ something about God. Just as a piece of artwork points to an artist, the created world points to a Creator. Evidence for God is not hidden away; it is visible above us every day.
elements.questionHint: If the heavens are said to ’declare’ something, what does that imply about what an observer of the natural world should be able to conclude?

elements.passageText1: <sup>13</sup>Kai ne ka halicci kowace gaɓa ta jikina, <sup>14</sup>Ina yabonka gama kai abin tsoro ne,
elements.passageText3: <sup>13</sup>Gama ka halicci ciki-cikina; <sup>14</sup>Ina yabonka domin yadda ka yi ni abin tsoro ne da kuma abin mamaki;
elements.passageText5: <sup>13</sup>Certainly you made my mind and heart; you wove me together in my mother’s womb. <sup>14</sup>I will give you thanks because your deeds are awesome and amazing. You knew me thoroughly;

elements.question1: Menene bayanin mai zabura game da 'saƙa' yake nunawa game da hannun Allah a rayuwar ɗan adam?
elements.question2: Hol ko siftorde Jabuura’en nde ’woɗɗude’ hollirta ko fayti e naatgol Alla e nguurndam aadee?
elements.question3: What does the psalmist’s description of being ’woven together’ suggest about God’s involvement in human life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The image of being ’woven together’ suggests that human life is not an accident or a random biological event, but an intentional, careful act of craftsmanship by God. Just as a weaver works deliberately, thread by thread, God was personally involved in forming each person. This points to God’s intimate knowledge of and care for every individual human being from the very beginning of their existence.
elements.questionHint: Think about what weaving involves — would you describe it as a careless, random process, or a deliberate and careful one? What does that tell you about how God formed you?

elements.passageText1: <sup>30</sup>Yesu ya yi waɗansu mu'ujizai da yawa dabam dabam a gaban almajiran, waɗanda ba a rubuta a littafin nan ba. <sup>31</sup>Amma an rubuta waɗannan ne, domin ku ba da gaskiya Yesu shi ne Almasihu, Ɗan Allah, ta wurin gaskatawa kuma ku sami rai a cikin sunansa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>30</sup>Yesu ya yi waɗansu abubuwan banmamaki da yawa a gaban almajiransa da ba a rubuta a wannan littafi ba. <sup>31</sup>An dai rubuta waɗannan ne don ku gaskata Yesu ne Kiristi, Ɗan Allah kuma ta wurin gaskatawa ku sami rai a cikin sunansa.
elements.passageText4: <sup>30</sup>30 Yeesu waɗii ko hayɗinii feere‑feere ɗuɗɗum yeeso tokkuɓemo ɗum windaaka nder ɗerewol ngo'ol. <sup>31</sup>31 Ammaa ɗu'um ɗum windaa ngam koolooɗon Yeesu woni Almasiihu Ɓiɗɗo Allah, nden laawol hoolaakimo keɓon yonki ki re'ataa barka inde maako.
elements.passageText5: <sup>30</sup>Now Jesus performed many other miraculous signs in the presence of the disciples, which are not recorded in this book. <sup>31</sup>But these are recorded so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name.

elements.question1: Me ya sa Yohanna ya ce ya rubuta waɗannan alamu? Me wannan ya gaya mana game da manufar Littafi Mai Tsarki?
elements.question2: Hol ko waɗi Yuhanna wiyde o winndii ɗee maandeeji? Hol ko ɗum anndini en e faandaare Biibal ngal?
elements.question3: Why does John say he recorded these signs? What does this tell us about the purpose of the Bible?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: John says he recorded these signs so that readers would come to believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and through that belief find life in his name. This tells us that the Bible is not merely a historical record or a collection of moral teachings — it has an intentional, evangelistic purpose. It is written to bring people to faith and, through faith, to life.
elements.questionHint: John tells us directly what outcome he was hoping for in his readers — can you spot the two goals he mentions, and how are they connected to each other?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>A zamanin dā, Allah ya yi wa kakannin kakanninmu magana ta hanyoyi masu yawa iri iri, ta bakin annabawa, <sup>2</sup>amma a zamanin nan na ƙarshe, sai ya yi mana magana ta wurin Ɗansa, wanda ya sa magājin kome, wanda ta wurinsa ne kuma ya halicci duniya. <sup>3</sup>Shi ne hasken ɗaukakar Allah, ainihin kamannin zatinsa kuma, shi kuma yana a riƙe da dukan abubuwa ta ikon faɗarsa. Bayan da ya tsarkake zunubanmu, sai ya zauna a dama da Maɗaukaki a can Sama.
elements.passageText3: <sup>1</sup>A dā Allah ya yi wa kakanni-kakanninmu magana ta bakin annabawa a lokuta dabam-dabam da kuma a hanyoyi iri-iri, <sup>2</sup>amma a waɗannan kwanaki a ƙarshe ya yi magana da mu ta wurin Ɗansa, wanda ya naɗa magājin dukan abubuwa, wanda kuma ta wurinsa ya halicci duniya. <sup>3</sup>Ɗan shi ne hasken ɗaukakar Allah, shi ne kuma ainihin kamanninsa, yana riƙe da dukan abubuwa ta wurin kalmarsa mai iko. Bayan ya yi tsarkakewa don zunubai, sai ya zauna a hannun daman Maɗaukaki a sama.
elements.passageText4: <sup>1</sup>1 Ndenno Allah metanii maama'en meeɗen nde ɗuɗɗum e ɗate ɗuɗɗe daga konduɗe annabo'en. <sup>2</sup>2 Ammaa nder balɗe ɗe'e ɗe re'uki duuniyaaru o metiranii'en Ɓiɗɗo maako, mo o suɓi warta donoowo koo ɗume. Ɗonmaa barka maako Allah tagi duuniyaaru. <sup>3</sup>3 E mo holla jayngol teddungal Allah, nden nder koo ɗume e mo nandi e Allah. E mo jogorii koo ɗume wolde maako goodunde bawɗe. Gaɗa o waɗii laawol yaafaaki hakke, o jooɗii ton dow to jungo nyaamo ngo Allah Joomu Mangu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>After God spoke long ago in various portions and in various ways to our ancestors through the prophets, <sup>2</sup>in these last days he has spoken to us in a son, whom he appointed heir of all things, and through whom he created the world. <sup>3</sup>The Son is the radiance of his glory and the representation of his essence, and he sustains all things by his powerful word, and so when he had accomplished cleansing for sins, he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high.

elements.question1: Ta yaya Allah ya yi mana magana, bisa ga wannan nassin? Me yake gaya mana game da dangantakar Yesu da Allah?
elements.question2: Hol no Alla haaldirta e men, fawaade e ndee ɗoo tonngoode? Hol ko nde haalanta en e jokkondiral Iisaa e Alla?
elements.question3: How has God spoken to us, according to this passage? What does it tell us about Jesus’ relationship to God?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: According to this passage, God spoke in the past through the prophets in many different ways, but has now spoken definitively and finally through His Son, Jesus. The passage makes extraordinary claims about Jesus’ relationship to God: he is the ’radiance of his glory’ and the ’representation of his essence’, meaning that to see Jesus is to see what God is truly like. Jesus also created the world, sustains all things, and has now accomplished the cleansing of sins — pointing to a unique divine identity.
elements.questionHint: The passage contrasts how God spoke ’long ago’ with how he has spoken ’in these last days’ — what is different about the two ways, and what does the description of the Son in verse 3 tell you about how closely he is linked to God himself?

elements.text1: Yaya kamanin Allah yake?
elements.text3: What is God like?

elements.passageText1: <sup>3</sup># W.Yah 4.8  Suna ta kiran junansu suna cewa,
elements.passageText3: <sup>3</sup>Suna kiran juna,
elements.passageText5: And one called to another and said: 3’Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of Heaven’s Armies! His majestic splendor fills the entire earth!’

elements.question3: What does it mean for God to be described as ’holy’? Why do you think that word is repeated three times?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: For God to be ’holy’ means He is utterly set apart — morally perfect, pure, and completely unlike anything in the created world. Holiness is not just one quality among many but speaks to His absolute otherness and majesty. The threefold repetition (‘holy, holy, holy’) is the strongest form of emphasis in Hebrew — it is not just saying God is very holy, but that holiness defines His very being to the highest possible degree. No other attribute of God is praised in this tripled way in Scripture.
elements.questionHint: In everyday language, if you want to stress how extremely something is true, what do you do — and how might that same idea apply to why the angels repeat the word ’holy’ three times rather than just once?

Amma daga yanzu kun san shi, kun kuma gan shi.” <sup>8</sup>Sai Filibus ya ce masa, “Ya Ubangiji, ka nuna mana Uban, bukatarmu ta biya ke nan.” <sup>9</sup>Yesu ya ce masa, “Na daɗe tare da ku haka, amma har yanzu ba ka san ni ba, Filibus? Duk wanda ya gan ni, ai, ya ga Uban. Yaya za ka ce, ‘Nuna mana Uban’”?

Daga yanzu, kun san shi kun kuma gan shi.” <sup>8</sup>Filibus ya ce, “Ubangiji, ka nuna mana Uban, wannan kuwa zai biya bukatarmu.” <sup>9</sup>Yesu ya amsa ya ce,  “Ba san ni ba, Filibus, duk da daɗewan nan da na yi tare da ku? Duk wanda ya gan ni, ai, ya ga Uban. Yaya za ka ce, ‘Ka nuna mana Uban’?
elements.passageText4: <sup>7</sup>7 O wi'iɓe, “Daa on andiyam, daa on andi Nyaako am Mo Dow maa. Daga jooni on andimo on ngi'iimo.” <sup>8</sup>8 Filibus wi'imo, “Moodibbo, hollumin Nyaako Mo Dow on, non heƴiimin.” <sup>9</sup>9 Yeesu nootii wi'i, “Filibus, ko neeɓi e mi wondi e mooɗon, ammaa fuu e non a andaayam? Koo moye giiɗoyam yi'ii Nyaako Mo Dow. Ngam ɗume mbi'ataa mi holla'on Nyaako Mo Dow?
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>If you have known me, you will know my Father too. And from now on you do know him and have seen him.’ <sup>8</sup>Philip said to him, ‘Lord, show us the Father, and we will be content.’ <sup>9</sup>Jesus replied, ‘Have I been with you for so long, and you have not known me, Philip? The person who has seen me has seen the Father! How can you say, "Show us the Father"?’

elements.question3: What does Jesus claim about the relationship between Himself and the Father? What does that mean for how we understand God?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus makes a remarkable claim: that to have seen Him is to have seen the Father. He is not merely a messenger from God, or someone who teaches about God — He is the very image and presence of God. This means that if we want to know what God is like, we do not need to look further than Jesus: His compassion, His honesty, His treatment of people, His willingness to lay down His life — all of these reveal the character of God the Father.
elements.questionHint: Philip asks to be shown the Father as if the Father were somewhere else — why does Jesus find this question so surprising, and what does His answer imply about where the Father can actually be found?

elements.passageText1:
<sup>2</sup>Kafin a kafa tuddai, <sup>4</sup>  # 
 2Bit 3.8 
  A gare ka shekara dubu, kamar kwana ɗaya ne,
elements.passageText3: <sup>2</sup>Kafin a haifi duwatsu <sup>4</sup>Gama shekaru dubu a gabanka
elements.passageText5: <sup>2</sup>Even before the mountains were born, or you gave birth to the earth and the world, from eternity to eternity you are God. <sup>4</sup>Yes, in your eyes a thousand years are like yesterday that quickly passes, or like one of the divisions of the nighttime.

elements.question3: What does this passage tell us about God’s relationship to time?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: This passage tells us that God exists outside of and before time itself — He was God before the earth or mountains existed, and He will continue to be God forever. Verse 4 shows that God does not experience time as we do: what feels like a thousand years to us is like a single day to Him. God is not constrained by time the way we are; He sees all of history as present to Him at once, which means He is never caught off guard, never in a hurry, and never late.
elements.questionHint: The psalmist says God existed even before the mountains or the earth were ’born’ — what does that tell you about whether God had a beginning, and how might that affect the way He experiences time compared to us?

elements.passageText1: <sup>17</sup>Kowace kyakkyawar baiwa, da kowace cikakkiyar kyauta, daga Sama suke, sun sauko ne daga wurin Uban haskoki, wanda ba sauyawa ko wata alamar sākewa a gare shi faufau.
elements.passageText3: <sup>17</sup>Kowane abu mai kyau da kuma kowace cikakkiyar kyauta daga sama suke, sun sauko ne daga wurin Uban haskoki, wanda ba sauyawa ko wata alamar sākewa a gare shi faufau.
elements.passageText4: <sup>17</sup>17 Koo ngale dokkal bongal e koo ɗume caahu jamum fuu daga to Allah Nyaako Mo Dow ɗum ƴiwoyta, taguɗo naange e lewru e koode. Kanko o waylitataako, o nandaa e ɗowdi goosotoondi.
elements.passageText5: <sup>17</sup>All generous giving and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or the slightest hint of change.

elements.question3: What does it mean that there is ’no variation or the slightest hint of change’ in God? Why is that significant?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: It means that God’s character, His love, His promises, and His goodness never shift or fluctuate. Unlike human relationships — where people can be kind one day and cold the next — God is completely consistent. This is enormously significant because it means we can trust Him fully. His love for us today is exactly as strong as it was yesterday and will be tomorrow. His promises do not expire or get revised. We never have to worry that God has changed His mind about us.
elements.questionHint: Think about a time when someone’s changing moods or broken promises made it hard to trust them — how might God being completely unchanging make a difference to how you relate to Him?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>Ina zan tafi in tsere wa Ruhunka? <sup>8</sup>Idan na hau cikin samaniya kana can, <sup>9</sup>In na tashi sama, na tafi, na wuce gabas, <sup>10</sup>Kana can domin ka bi da ni,
elements.passageText3: <sup>7</sup>Ina zan tafi daga Ruhunka? <sup>8</sup>In na haura zuwa sammai, kana a can; <sup>9</sup>In na tashi a fikafikan safiya, <sup>10</sup>can ma hannunka zai bishe ni,
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>Where can I go to escape your Spirit? Where can I flee to escape your presence? <sup>8</sup>If I were to ascend to heaven, you would be there. If I were to sprawl out in Sheol, there you would be. <sup>9</sup>If I were to fly away on the wings of the dawn, and settle down on the other side of the ocean, <sup>10</sup>even there your hand would guide me, your right hand would grab hold of me.

elements.question3: What does this passage tell us about God’s presence? Is that a comforting or a challenging thought for you?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: This passage tells us that God is omnipresent — there is literally nowhere in the universe, from the heights of heaven to the depths of Sheol, where God is absent. His presence is inescapable. Whether that is comforting or challenging likely depends on how we feel about God. For someone who trusts God and longs for His help, it is deeply comforting — no matter how far or alone we feel, His hand is still guiding us. But it can also feel challenging or exposing, because it means nothing in our lives is hidden from Him.
elements.questionHint: The psalmist lists extreme opposites — heaven and Sheol, near and far — to make a point about God’s presence. What conclusion is he building towards, and does knowing God is always present feel like good news or an uncomfortable truth to you personally?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Wanda ba ya ƙauna, bai san Allah ba sam, domin Allah shi ne ƙauna.
elements.passageText3: <sup>8</sup>Duk wanda ba ya ƙauna, bai san Allah ba, domin Allah ƙauna ne.
elements.passageText4: <sup>8</sup>8 Fuu mo walaa yidde, o andaa Allah, ngam Allah o yidde.
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>The person who does not love does not know God, because God is love.

elements.question3: John says ’God is love’ — not just that God loves, but that love defines who He is. What difference does that make?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Saying God loves is saying something He does — an action He can choose or withhold. But saying God is love means love is inseparable from His very being; He cannot stop being loving any more than He can stop being God. It means His love is not a response to us being lovable — it flows from who He is. This also means that love is not just a feeling God has but the core of His identity, which gives us grounds to trust that His love is permanent, unconditional, and inexhaustible.
elements.questionHint: Consider the difference between saying ’a teacher is kind’ and ’a teacher sometimes acts kindly’ — which says more about their core identity, and how does that distinction apply to what John is claiming about God?

elements.text1: Tayaya mu ka san cewar Allah yana ƙaunar mu?
elements.text3: How do we know that God loves us?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Amma kuwa Allah yana tabbatar mana da ƙaunar da yake yi mana, wato tun muna masu zunubi, Almasihu ya mutu dominmu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>8</sup>Amma Allah ya nuna mana ƙaunarsa a wannan. Tun muna masu zunubi tukuna, Kiristi ya mutu dominmu.
elements.passageText4: <sup>8</sup>8 Ammaa laawol ngol Allah hollii'en yidde maako woni, carel ko ngonɗen nder hakke, Almasiihu maayii ngam meeɗen.
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>But God demonstrates his own love for us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

elements.question3: How does Paul say God has demonstrated His love? What is striking about the timing — ‘while we were still sinners’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says God demonstrated His love through the death of Christ on our behalf. What is especially striking is the timing: God did not wait for us to clean up our lives, earn His favour, or become worthy of His love. Christ died for us while we were still sinners — still in rebellion against God. This overturns the human assumption that love must be deserved. God’s love is not a reward for good behaviour; it is a free, initiating love given to those who had done nothing to merit it.
elements.questionHint: In most human relationships, we tend to show kindness to people who have been kind to us — so what is surprising or unexpected about the specific moment Paul says God chose to demonstrate His love?

elements.passageText1: <sup>9</sup>Ta haka aka bayyana ƙaunar Allah gare mu, da Allah ya aiko da Ɗansa makaɗaici a duniya, domin mu sami rai madawwami ta wurinsa. <sup>10</sup>Ta haka ƙauna take, wato, ba mu ne muka ƙaunaci Allah ba, sai dai shi ne ya ƙaunace mu, ya aiko Ɗansa, hadayar sulhu saboda a gafarta zunubanmu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>9</sup>Ga yadda Allah ya nuna ƙaunarsa a cikinmu, Allah ya aiki makaɗaici Ɗansa zuwa duniya domin mu rayu ta wurinsa. <sup>10</sup>Wannan ita ce ƙauna, wato, ba mu ne muka ƙaunaci Allah ba, sai dai shi ne ya ƙaunace mu, ya kuma aiko Ɗansa yă zama hadaya ta kafara saboda zunubanmu.
elements.passageText4: <sup>9</sup>9 Allah hollii'en yidde muuɗum nde o liloyi Ɓiɗɗo maako go'oto tan mo o woodi nder duuniyaaru, ngam keɓen yonki ki re'ataa barka Ɓiɗɗo on. <sup>10</sup>10 Raa no yidde nde goonga wa'i, naa' enen ngiɗi Allah, ammaa Allah yiɗi'en o liloyi Ɓiɗɗo maako ngam warta cakkiri yaafaaki hakke meeɗen.
elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>By this the love of God is revealed in us: that God has sent his one and only Son into the world so that we may live through him. <sup>10</sup>In this is love: not that we have loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the atoning sacrifice for our sins.

elements.question3: According to John, who took the initiative in this relationship — God or us? What does that tell us about the nature of God’s love?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: John is explicit that God took the initiative — love is defined here not as our love for God, but as His love for us. God moved first. He sent His Son before we sought Him, to deal with the sin that separated us from Him. This tells us that God’s love is not reactive or conditional — it is originating and unconditional. We do not draw God’s love out of Him by our devotion; He loved us freely and acted on that love at great cost, sending His only Son as a sacrifice.
elements.questionHint: John says ’not that we have loved God, but that he loved us’ — who does he say made the first move, and what practical difference might it make to you personally to know that the relationship started with God, not with you?

elements.text1: Gaskiya al'amari
elements.text3: Key truths

elements.text1:
Wauta/Wawanci ne a yi ma tunanin cewar babu Allah

'Wawa ya ce a zuciyar sa, "Ba Allah."'

Zabura 14:1a

Allah ya ƙaunar mu kuma akwai buƙatar mu ma kula da lura da ƙaunarsa

'Duba irin gagarumar ƙaunar da Uba ya nuna mana, da cewar a kira mu ƴaƴan Allah! Wanda hakan kuma muke!'

1 Yahaya 3:1a
elements.text3:
It is foolish to think that there is no God.

"The fool says in his heart, ‘There is no God.’" — Psalm 14:1a

God loves us and longs that we might respond to His love.

elements.text3: To think about

elements.question1: Tayaya za ka/ki iya kokarin gamsar da tabbatar wa ɗan'uwa ko aboki akwai Allah?
elements.question3: How would you try to convince a friend that God does actually exist?

chapters.chapterTitle: Jesus Christ, the Son of God

elements.text1: Akwai banbanci ga yarda wasu suka cewar, Wai Yesu ba tun fil'azal yake ba amma ya kasance ne kawai lokacin da Maryamu ta haife shi a Baitalami. Yesu yana nan tun kafin farkon komai.
elements.text3: Many people think of Jesus as a great teacher or a good man who lived two thousand years ago. But the Bible presents Someone far greater. Surprisingly, Jesus did not begin at Bethlehem — He has always existed. In this study we look at who Jesus really is.

elements.text1: Me muka sani game da Yesu kafin zuwansa duniya?
elements.text3: What do we know about Jesus before He came to earth?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>Tun fil'azal akwai Kalma, Kalman nan kuwa tare da Allah yake, Kalman nan kuwa Allah ne. <sup>2</sup>Shi ne tun fil'azal yake tare da Allah. <sup>3</sup>Dukan abubuwa sun kasance ta gare shi ne, ba kuma abin da ya kasance na abubuwan da suka kasance, sai ta game da shi.
elements.passageText3: <sup>1</sup>Tun farar farawa akwai Kalma, Kalman kuwa yana nan tare da Allah, Kalman kuwa Allah ne. <sup>2</sup>Yana nan tare da Allah tun farar farawa. <sup>3</sup>Ta wurin Kalman ne aka halicci dukan abubuwa. In ba tare da shi ba, babu abin da aka yi wanda aka yi.
elements.passageText4: <sup>1</sup>1 Daga ko duuniyaaru tagee bi'eteeɗo Wolde e ɗon, e mo wondi e Allah, nden o Allah. <sup>2</sup>2 Daga ko duuniyaaru tagee bi'eteeɗo Wolde on e wondi e Allah. <sup>3</sup>3 O tagii koo ɗume. Walaa goɗɗum nder fuu ko tagaa ɗum um tagiraayi laawol maako.
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was fully God. <sup>2</sup>The Word was with God in the beginning. <sup>3</sup>All things were created by him, and apart from him not one thing was created that has been created.
elements.note: In this passage the ’Word’ is referring to Jesus.

elements.question3: John calls Jesus ’the Word’. What does this passage tell us about where Jesus was before He came to earth?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Before coming to earth, Jesus — the Word — already existed. He was with God in the beginning, meaning He has always been in close relationship with the Father. More than that, He was fully God Himself. John also tells us that all things were created through Him, meaning Jesus was not a created being who appeared at some point in history, but the eternal Creator who was present before anything else existed.
elements.questionHint: John says the Word was ’in the beginning’ — does that suggest Jesus had a starting point, or that He was already there before anything began? What else does John say about His role before creation?

elements.passageText1: <sup>15</sup>Shi ne surar Allah marar ganuwa, magāji ne tun ba a halicci kome ba, <sup>16</sup>don ta gare shi ne aka halicci dukkan kome, abubuwan da suke a sama da abubuwan da suke a ƙasa, masu ganuwa da marasa ganuwa, ko kursiyai, ko masu mulki, ko masarauta, ko masu iko, wato, dukkan abubuwa an halicce su ne ta wurinsa, shi ne kuma makomarsu. <sup>17</sup>Shi ne a gaba da dukkan abubuwa, dukkan abubuwa kuma shi ne yake riƙe da su.
elements.passageText3: <sup>15</sup>Kiristi shi ne surar da ake gani na Allah wanda ba a iya gani, ɗan fari a kan dukan halitta. <sup>16</sup>Ta wurinsa aka halicci dukan abu, abubuwan da suke cikin sama da abubuwan da suke a duniya, waɗanda ake gani, da waɗanda ba a gani, ko kursiyoyi ko ikoki ko masu mulki ko hukumomi; an halicci dukan abu ta wurinsa da kuma dominsa ne. <sup>17</sup>Ya kasance kafin dukan abu, kuma a cikinsa ne dukan abubuwa suke a harhaɗe.
elements.passageText4: <sup>15</sup>15 Ngikku Almasiihu mo ngi'eten ɗum go'o e ngikku Allah mo yi'ataake. E mo ɗon ko Allah taga koo ɗume, o mawɗo dow koo ɗume ko tagaa. <sup>16</sup>16 Barka maako Allah tagi koo ɗume gonɗum dow e ley, ko yi'etee e ko yi'ataake, hawti e malaa'ika'en, e ginni, e koo ɗume gooduɗum laamu e bawɗe. Koo ɗume kanko tagiɗum, nden ngam maako koo ɗume tagaa. <sup>17</sup>17 Almasiihu e ɗon ko koo ɗume tagee, barka maako koo ɗume e yaha yeeso.
elements.passageText5: <sup>15</sup>He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation. <sup>16</sup>For by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities—all things were created through him and for him. <sup>17</sup>And he is before all things, and in him all things hold together.

elements.question3: What does Paul say Jesus’ role was in creation? What does verse 17 suggest about His ongoing relationship to the universe?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says that all things — in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible — were created by Jesus, through Jesus, and for Jesus. His role in creation was total: nothing came into existence apart from Him. Verse 17 then goes further, saying that in Him all things ’hold together’. This means Jesus is not just the originator of creation but its ongoing sustainer — the universe continues to exist and cohere because of Him. He is not a distant figure who started things and stepped away; He is actively involved in holding everything together at this very moment.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses three prepositions in verse 16 — all things were created ’by’, ‘through’, and ’for’ Jesus. What does adding those three together suggest about the scope of His involvement in creation, and how does verse 17 extend that idea into the present?

elements.text1: Don me aka kira shi Yesu?
elements.text3: Why was He called Jesus?

elements.passageText1: <sup>21</sup># Luk 1.31  Za ta haifi ɗa, za ka kuma sa masa suna Yesu, domin shi ne zai ceci mutanensa daga zunubansu.”
elements.passageText3: <sup>21</sup>Za tă haifi ɗa, za ka kuma sa masa suna Yesu, # 1.21  Yesu  da Girik shi ne  Yoshuwa da Ibraniyanci,  wanda yake nufin  Ubangiji  yana ceto.  gama shi ne zai ceci mutanensa daga zunubansu.”
elements.passageText4: <sup>21</sup>21 O danyay ɓiɗɗo gorko nden a noddaymo Yeesu, ngam o hisnay yimɓe maako daga hakke maɓɓe.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>21</sup>She will give birth to a son and you will name him Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins.

elements.question3: What does the name ’Jesus’ mean, and what does that tell us about the purpose of His coming?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The angel explains the name directly: Jesus means ’he will save his people from their sins’. The name itself is a mission statement. Jesus did not come primarily as a political liberator, a moral teacher, or a miracle worker — He came to deal with the problem of sin. His name tells us that rescue from sin is the central purpose of His life, and that this rescue was planned and announced before He was even born.
elements.questionHint: The angel gives a reason for the name with the word ’for’ — what specific problem does He say Jesus will come to solve, and does that surprise you given what people might have expected a Saviour to do?

elements.text1: Tayaya muka san gaskiyar cewa Yesu cikkaken mutum ne?
elements.text3: How do we know that Jesus is truly human?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>Sai ta haifi ɗanta na fari, ta rufi shi da zanen goyo, ta kuma kwantar da shi a wani komi dabbobi, don ba su sami ɗaki a masaukin ba. <sup>40</sup>Ɗan yaron kuwa ya girma, ya kawo ƙarfi, yana mai matuƙar hikima. Alherin Allah kuwa yana tare da shi.
elements.passageText3: <sup>7</sup>ta kuwa haifi ɗanta na fari. Ta nannaɗe shi a zane ta kuma kwantar da shi cikin wani kwami, don ba su sami ɗaki a masauƙi ba. <sup>40</sup>Yaron kuwa ya yi girma ya yi ƙarfi; ya cika da hikima, alherin Allah kuwa yana bisansa.
elements.passageText4: <sup>7</sup>7 Sey o danyi ɓiɗɗo gorko, afo maako, o saawimo nder wudere wooɗunde, o fukkinimo nder to ɗum waɗanta marle nyamndu, ngam walaa njayri nder suudu hoɓɓe. <sup>40</sup>40 Yeesu mawni, sembiɗi, faamu heewimo, nden barka Allah e dow maako.
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>And she gave birth to her firstborn son and wrapped him in strips of cloth and laid him in a manger, because there was no place for them in the inn. ... <sup>40</sup>The child grew and became strong, filled with wisdom, and the favor of God was upon him.

elements.question3: What details in these verses show that Jesus experienced a genuinely human childhood?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Verse 7 shows that Jesus was born in the ordinary, physical way — wrapped in cloth like any newborn infant and laid in a manger. He entered the world in vulnerability and poverty, not in heavenly splendour. Verse 40 then tells us He grew and became strong, and was filled with wisdom over time. This is the pattern of a normal human childhood: development, growth, and gradual maturation. Jesus was not born fully formed or all-knowing in His human experience — He grew up just as we do.
elements.questionHint: Verse 40 says Jesus ’grew’ and ’became strong’ — do those words suggest something that happened all at once or over time, and what does that tell you about whether His childhood was similar to ours?

elements.passageText1: <sup>2</sup>har kwana arba'in, Iblis yana gwada shi. A kwanakin nan bai ci kome ba. Da suka ƙare kuwa ya ji yunwa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>2</sup>inda kwana arba’in, Iblis ya gwada shi. Bai ci kome ba a cikin kwanakin nan, a ƙarshensu kuwa ya ji yunwa.
elements.passageText4: <sup>2</sup>2 Ton o waɗi balɗe 40 Ceyɗan e foondamo, o nyaamaayi goɗɗum nder balɗe ɗen. Nden gaɗa maaje, weelo nangimo.
elements.passageText5: Jesus was led by the Spirit in the wilderness, 2where for forty days he endured temptations from the devil. He ate nothing during those days, and when they were completed, he was famished.

elements.question3: What does it mean that Jesus ’was famished’? How does this confirm His humanity?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: That Jesus ’was famished’ means He experienced real, intense physical hunger — the kind that comes from forty days without food. This is not a metaphor; it is a physiological reality. A purely divine being would not experience bodily hunger, but Jesus did. This confirms that His human body was genuine and subject to the same physical limitations as ours: He needed food, He felt the pain of going without it, and His body was weakened by the experience. His humanity was not a costume — it was real.
elements.questionHint: Think about what hunger feels like after missing just one meal — what does experiencing it after forty days tell you about whether Jesus had a real human body with real physical needs?

elements.passageText1: <sup>23</sup>Da ya shiga jirgi, almajiransa suka bi shi. <sup>24</sup>Sai ga wani babban hadiri ya taso a teku, har raƙuman ruwa suka fara shan kan jirgin, amma yana barci.
elements.passageText3: <sup>23</sup>Sai ya shiga jirgin ruwa, almajiransa kuwa suka bi shi. <sup>24</sup>Ba zato ba tsammani, sai wani babban hadari ya taso a tafkin, har raƙuman ruwan suka sha kan jirgin. Amma Yesu yana barci.
elements.passageText4: <sup>23</sup>23 Yeesu natti komiwal, tokkuɓemo maa tokkimo. <sup>24</sup>24 Sey hendu cembindu ummoyii dow mbeela ngan, geelooɗi ndiyam puɗɗi piiki komiwal ngal, ndiyam natti nder maagal haa naaki ngal ɓadii heewa ndiyam, ammaa Yeesu e nder maagal e ɗaanii.
elements.passageText5: <sup>23</sup>As he got into the boat, his disciples followed him. <sup>24</sup>And suddenly a great storm developed on the sea, so that the waves began to swamp the boat; but he was asleep.

elements.question3: What is humanly significant about the detail that Jesus was asleep during the storm?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Sleep is one of the most fundamental marks of being human — it reflects physical tiredness and the body’s need for rest. The fact that Jesus was asleep in the boat, even in the middle of a violent storm, tells us He had been working hard enough to be genuinely exhausted. He was not pretending to be tired; He actually needed rest. This small detail quietly but powerfully confirms that His humanity was authentic: He got tired just as we do.
elements.questionHint: Why do human beings need to sleep at all, and what does needing sleep tell you about the kind of body a person has — could someone who never truly got tired fall into a deep sleep during a dangerous storm?

elements.passageText1: <sup>32</sup>Sai soja suka zo suka karya ƙafafun na farkon nan, da kuma na ɗayan da aka gicciye tare da shi. <sup>33</sup>Amma da suka zo wurin Yesu, suka kuma ga ya riga ya mutu, ba su karya ƙafafunsa ba.
elements.passageText3: <sup>32</sup>Sai sojoji suka zo suka karya ƙafafun mutum na farko da aka gicciye tare da Yesu, da kuma na ɗayan. <sup>33</sup>Amma sa’ad da suka zo kan Yesu suka tarar ya riga ya mutu, ba su karya ƙafafunsa ba.
elements.passageText4: <sup>32</sup>32 Ngam non sooji'en ɓen njahi to gaafaale ɗen. Ɓe ngewi koyɗe arandeejo, nden ɓe ngewi koyɗe ɗiɗaɓo on. <sup>33</sup>33 Ammaa nde ɓe ngari to Yeesu ɓe ngi'i o yaadake o maayii, ɓe ngewaayi koyɗe maako.
elements.passageText5: <sup>32</sup>So the soldiers came and broke the legs of the two men who had been crucified with Jesus, first the one and then the other. <sup>33</sup>But when they came to Jesus and saw that he was already dead, they did not break his legs.

elements.question3: Why is it significant that Jesus actually died on the cross? What does John want us to understand here?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: John records that the soldiers — professionals whose job was to ensure death — confirmed that Jesus was already dead. They had no reason to spare Him and every reason to make sure. The fact that He was genuinely dead is significant on two levels. First, it confirms His full humanity: a divine being who merely appeared human could not truly die. Second, it establishes that the resurrection was a real resurrection from actual death, not a recovery from unconsciousness. John is providing eyewitness evidence that the death was real, so that the reader can trust that the resurrection was equally real.
elements.questionHint: The soldiers broke the legs of the others to speed their deaths — so why is it significant that they did not need to do this to Jesus, and what were they effectively confirming by leaving Him alone?

elements.text1: Tayaya muka san gaskiyar Yesu Allah ne?
elements.text3: How do we know that Jesus is truly God?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>Tun fil'azal akwai Kalma, Kalman nan kuwa tare da Allah yake, Kalman nan kuwa Allah ne.
elements.passageText3: <sup>1</sup>Tun farar farawa akwai Kalma, Kalman kuwa yana nan tare da Allah, Kalman kuwa Allah ne.
elements.passageText4: <sup>1</sup>1 Daga ko duuniyaaru tagee bi'eteeɗo Wolde e ɗon, e mo wondi e Allah, nden o Allah.
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was fully God.

elements.question3: John says the Word ’was fully God’. How does this verse set the foundation for understanding who Jesus is?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: John opens his Gospel with a deliberate echo of Genesis — ‘In the beginning’ — and immediately places Jesus (the Word) there, before anything else existed. He then makes two statements in one breath: the Word was with God (pointing to a distinct, personal relationship) and the Word was fully God (pointing to a shared divine nature). This verse establishes from the outset that Jesus cannot be understood as merely a great man or even a lesser divine being — He is fully God, and the rest of John’s Gospel is written to demonstrate what that means.
elements.questionHint: John says the Word was both ’with God’ and ’was fully God’ — do those two statements contradict each other, or could they together be pointing to something about Jesus that is more complex than a simple either/or?

elements.passageText1: <sup>5</sup>Da Yesu ya ga bangaskiyarsu sai ya ce wa shanyayyen, “Ɗana, an gafarta maka zunubanka.” <sup>6</sup>To, waɗansu malaman Attaura na nan zaune, suna ta wuswasi a zuciya tasu, <sup>7</sup>suna cewa, “Don me mutumin nan yake faɗar haka? Ai, sāɓo yake! Wa yake da ikon gafarta zunubi banda Allah kaɗai?” <sup>8</sup>Nan da nan, da Yesu ya gane a ransa suna ta wuswasi haka a zuci, sai ya ce musu, “Don me kuke wuswasi haka a zuciyarku? <sup>9</sup>Wanne ya fi sauƙi, a ce wa shanyayyen, ‘An gafarta maka zunubanka’, ko kuwa a ce, ‘Tashi ka ɗauki shimfiɗarka ka yi tafiya’? <sup>10</sup>Amma don ku sakankance Ɗan Mutum na da ikon gafarta zunubi a duniya” sai ya ce wa shanyayyen, <sup>11</sup>“Na ce maka, tashi ka ɗauki shimfiɗarka ka tafi gida.” <sup>12</sup>Sai ya tashi nan da nan, ya ɗauki shimfiɗarsa, ya fita a gaban idon kowa, har suka yi mamaki duka, suka ɗaukaka Allah suna cewa, “Ba mu taɓa ganin irin haka ba!”
elements.passageText3: <sup>5</sup>Da Yesu ya ga bangaskiyarsu, sai ya ce wa shanyayyen,  “Saurayi, an gafarta zunubanka.” <sup>6</sup>To, waɗansu malaman dokoki da suke zaune a wurin, suka yi tunani a zuciyarsu, suna cewa, <sup>7</sup>“Me ya sa mutumin nan yake magana haka? Yana saɓo! Wane ne zai iya gafarta zunubai, in ba Allah kaɗai ba?” <sup>8</sup>Nan da nan, Yesu ya gane a ruhunsa abin da suke tunani a zuciyarsu, sai ya ce musu,  “Me ya sa kuke tunanin waɗannan abubuwa? <sup>9</sup>Wanne ya fi sauƙi, a ce wa shanyayyen, ‘An gafarta zunubanka,’ ko a ce, ‘Tashi, ɗauki tabarmarka ka yi tafiya’? <sup>10</sup>Amma don ku san cewa, Ɗan Mutum yana da iko a duniya yă gafarta zunubai.”  Sai ya ce wa shanyayyen, <sup>11</sup>“Ina ce maka, tashi, ɗauki tabarmarka, ka tafi gida.” <sup>12</sup>Sai ya tashi, ya ɗauki tabarmarsa, ya yi tafiyarsa a gabansu duka. Wannan ya ba wa kowa mamaki, suka kuma ɗaukaka Allah suna cewa, “Ba mu taɓa ganin abu haka ba!”
elements.passageText4: <sup>5</sup>5 Nde Yeesu yi'i hoolaare maɓɓe, o wi'i jaraaɗo on, “Higo, a yaafanaama hakkeeji maaɗa.” <sup>6</sup>6 Woɓɓe moodiɓɓe Attawra e njooɗii ɗon e ɓe nyuma nder ɓerɗe maɓɓe, <sup>7</sup>7 e ɓe mbi'a, “Ngam ɗume neɗɗo o'o metirta nihi? E mo fottidina hoore maako e Allah! Moye waawata yaafaaki hakke to naa' Allah tan?” <sup>8</sup>8 Ɗon e ɗon Yeesu faami nder ɓernde muuɗum e ɓe nyuma dow fii ɗu'um nder ɓerɗe maɓɓe, o wi'iɓe, “Ngam ɗume nyumoton iri nyumooji ɗi'i nder ɓerɗe mooɗon? <sup>9</sup>9 Ko ɓuri hoyki, ɗum wi'a jaraaɗo o'o, ‘A yaafanaama hakke maaɗa,’ koo ɗum wi'amo, ‘Umma hoccu daago maaɗa, yaaɓu?’ <sup>10</sup>10 Ammaa e mi yiɗi andon Ɓii Neɗɗo e woodi laamu nder duuniyaaru yaafoo hakke.” Ngam non o wi'i jaraaɗo on, <sup>11</sup>11 “E mi wi'umaa, umma, koccaa daago maaɗa, kootaa wuro.” <sup>12</sup>12 Sey o ummii, o hocci daago maako, o wurtii, o dilli yeeso koo moye. Fii ɗu'um hayɗini fuu yimɓe gonɓe ɗon, ɓe manti Allah, ɓe mbi'i, “Min meeɗaayi yiiki iri fii ɗu'um!”
elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>When Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralytic, ‘Child, your sins are forgiven.’ <sup>6</sup>Now some of the experts in the law were sitting there, questioning in their hearts, <sup>7</sup>’Why does this man speak this way? He is blaspheming! Who can forgive sins but God alone?’ <sup>8</sup>Now immediately, when Jesus realized in his spirit that they were contemplating such thoughts, he said to them, ‘Why are you thinking such things in your hearts? <sup>9</sup>Which is easier, to say to the paralytic, "Your sins are forgiven," or to say, "Stand up, take your mat, and walk"? <sup>10</sup>But so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins’—he said to the paralytic— <sup>11</sup>’I tell you, stand up, take your mat, and go home.’ <sup>12</sup>And he stood up, took his mat immediately, and went out before them all. They were all amazed and glorified God, saying, ‘We’ve never seen anything like this!’

elements.question3: The religious leaders ask, ‘Who can forgive sins but God alone?’ How does Jesus’ response answer their question?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The religious leaders correctly identify that only God can forgive sins — and Jesus does not correct them on that point. Instead, He accepts the premise and then acts on it. He heals the paralysed man as visible proof that He has the authority to do what only God can do: forgive sins. Jesus is not claiming to forgive sins on God’s behalf, as a priest might; He is claiming to do it in His own right. His question — ‘which is easier?’ — is a trap: since both are impossible for a mere human, doing one publicly proves the authority to do the other. The miracle is His answer to their question.
elements.questionHint: The religious leaders’ logic was: only God forgives sins, therefore Jesus is blaspheming by claiming to do it — but what does Jesus do next, and how does that action respond to the charge rather than simply arguing against it?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Sai Filibus ya ce masa, “Ya Ubangiji, ka nuna mana Uban, bukatarmu ta biya ke nan.” <sup>9</sup>Yesu ya ce masa, “Na daɗe tare da ku haka, amma har yanzu ba ka san ni ba, Filibus? Duk wanda ya gan ni, ai, ya ga Uban. Yaya za ka ce, ‘Nuna mana Uban’”?
elements.passageText3: <sup>8</sup>Filibus ya ce, “Ubangiji, ka nuna mana Uban, wannan kuwa zai biya bukatarmu.” <sup>9</sup>Yesu ya amsa ya ce,  “Ba san ni ba, Filibus, duk da daɗewan nan da na yi tare da ku? Duk wanda ya gan ni, ai, ya ga Uban. Yaya za ka ce, ‘Ka nuna mana Uban’?
elements.passageText4: <sup>8</sup>8 Filibus wi'imo, “Moodibbo, hollumin Nyaako Mo Dow on, non heƴiimin.” <sup>9</sup>9 Yeesu nootii wi'i, “Filibus, ko neeɓi e mi wondi e mooɗon, ammaa fuu e non a andaayam? Koo moye giiɗoyam yi'ii Nyaako Mo Dow. Ngam ɗume mbi'ataa mi holla'on Nyaako Mo Dow?
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>Philip said to him, ‘Lord, show us the Father, and we will be content.’ <sup>9</sup>Jesus replied, ‘Have I been with you for so long, and you have not known me, Philip? The person who has seen me has seen the Father! How can you say, "Show us the Father"?’

elements.question3: What claim is Jesus making when He says ’whoever has seen me has seen the Father’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus is claiming that He is not merely a representative or messenger of the Father, but that He so perfectly reveals and embodies the Father that seeing Him is seeing the Father. This is an extraordinary claim: it means that if you want to know what God is like — His compassion, His honesty, His attitude to sinners, His willingness to suffer — you look at Jesus. Jesus does not point people away from Himself to find God; He says that God is found in Him. It is a direct claim to share in the divine nature and identity of the Father.
elements.questionHint: Philip wanted to be shown the Father as if Jesus and the Father were two entirely separate things to find — why does Jesus find this question so bewildering, and what does His surprise tell you about how closely He sees Himself as connected to the Father?

elements.passageText1: <sup>37</sup>Sai babban hadari mai iska ya taso, raƙuman ruwa kuma na ta fantsama cikin jirgin, har jirgin ya tasar wa cika da ruwa. <sup>38</sup>Yesu kuwa na daga ƙarshen baya na jirgin a kan matashi, yana barci. Sai suka tashe shi, suka ce masa, “Malam, za mu hallaka ba ka kula ba ko?” <sup>39</sup>Sai ya farka, ya tsawata wa iskar, ya kuma ce wa ruwan tekun, “Natsu! Ka yi shiru!” Sai iskar ta kwanta, wurin duk ya yi tsit. <sup>40</sup>Ya ce musu, “Don me kuka firgita haka? Har yanzu ba ku da bangaskiya ne?” <sup>41</sup>Sai suka tsorata, matuƙar tsoro, suka ce wa juna, “Wa ke nan kuma, wanda har iska da ruwan teku ma suke yi masa biyayya?”
elements.passageText3: <sup>37</sup>Sai babban hadari mai iska ya tashi, raƙuman ruwa suna ta bugun jirgin ruwan, har jirgin ruwan ya kusa yă cika da ruwa. <sup>38</sup>Yesu kuwa yana can baya, yana barci a kan katifa. Sai almajiran suka tashe shi suka ce masa, “Malam, ba ka kula ba ko mu nutse?” <sup>39</sup>Sai ya farka ya tsawata wa iskar, ya ce wa raƙuman ruwan,  “Shiru! Ku natsu!”  Sai iskar ta kwanta, kome kuma ya yi tsit. <sup>40</sup>Sai ya ce wa almajiransa,  “Don me kuke tsoro haka? Har yanzu ba ku da bangaskiya ne?” <sup>41</sup>Suka tsorata, suka ce wa juna, “Wane ne wannan? Har iska da raƙuman ruwa ma suna masa biyayya!”
elements.passageText4: <sup>37</sup>37 Sey iyeende e hendu cembindu ummoyii, geelooɗi ndiyam puɗɗi piiki komiwal ngal, ndiyam natti nder maagal haa naaki komiwal ngal ɓadii heewa ndiyam. <sup>38</sup>38 Yeesu boo e ton gaɗa komiwal ngal e ɗaanii dow ɓantirɗum hoore. Sey tokkuɓemo pindinimo mbi'imo, “Moodibbo, a wannaayi to en kalkii?” <sup>39</sup>39 Sey o ummii, o haɓani hendu ndun, o wi'i geelooɗi ndiyam ɗin, “Nde''itee! Alee!” Sey hendu ndun ali, koo ɗume de''iti. <sup>40</sup>40 O wi'i tokkuɓemo, “Ngam ɗume ngooduɗon kulol? Haa jooni on koolaakiyam?” <sup>41</sup>41 Ɓe kultori, ɓe ƴamƴamtiri, ɓe mbi'i, “Ɗum moye o'o? Haa hendu e geelooɗi ndiyam e ngaɗa ko o wi'i!”
elements.passageText5: <sup>37</sup>Now a great windstorm developed and the waves were breaking into the boat, so that the boat was nearly swamped. <sup>38</sup>But he was in the stern, sleeping on a cushion. They woke him up and said to him, ‘Teacher, don’t you care that we are about to die?’ <sup>39</sup>So he got up and rebuked the wind, and said to the sea, ‘Be quiet! Calm down!’ Then the wind stopped, and it was dead calm. <sup>40</sup>And he said to them, ‘Why are you cowardly? Do you still not have faith?’ <sup>41</sup>They were overwhelmed with fear and said to one another, ‘Who then is this? Even the wind and the sea obey him!’

elements.question3: The disciples ask ’Who then is this?’ after Jesus calms the storm. What answer does this miracle point to?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The disciples’ question is the right one, and the miracle itself supplies the answer. In the Old Testament, it is God who commands the seas and the winds — nature obeys its Creator. When Jesus rebukes the storm and it immediately calms, He is doing something that only God does. The disciples were not just impressed; they were ’overwhelmed with fear’ — a response that in Scripture is typically prompted by an encounter with the divine. The miracle points to the answer: this is God Himself in human form, the one who made the sea and therefore has authority over it.
elements.questionHint: In the Old Testament, who is described as having authority over the sea and storms? Knowing that, what conclusion does it seem like Mark wants the reader to draw from the disciples’ question?

elements.passageText1: <sup>25</sup>Yesu ya ce mata, “Ai, ni ne tashin matattu, ni ne kuma rai. Wanda ya gaskata da ni, ko ya mutu zai rayu. <sup>43</sup>Da ya faɗi haka ya ɗaga murya da ƙarfi ya ce, “Li'azaru fito!” <sup>44</sup>Sai mamacin ya fito, ƙafa da hannu a ɗaure da likkafani, fuska tasa kuma a naɗe da mayani. Yesu ya ce musu, “Ku kwance masa, ya tafi.”
elements.passageText3: <sup>25</sup>Yesu ya ce mata,  “Ni ne tashin matattu da kuma rai. Duk wanda ya gaskata da ni zai rayu, ko ya mutu; <sup>43</sup>Sa’ad da ya faɗa haka, sai Yesu ya yi kira da babbar murya ya ce,  “Lazarus, ka fito!” <sup>44</sup>Sai mamacin ya fito, hannuwansa da ƙafafunsa a daure da ƙyallayen lilin, fuskarsa kuwa an naɗe da mayafi.
elements.passageText4: <sup>25</sup>25 Yeesu wi'imo, “Min woni umminayɗo mayɓe, min woni kokkayɗo yonki. Koo moye kooliiɗoyam, koo o maayii, o fuɗɗitay o waɗa yonki. <sup>43</sup>43 Gaɗa o wi'ii non, o noddi e sembe, “Li'aajaru, wurtoya!” <sup>44</sup>44 Sey mayɗo on wurtoyii daga nder yenaande nden, ɓandu maako e taariraa wudere, juuɗe maako e koyɗe maako e kaɓɓiraa lefi, yeeso maako boo e taariraa lefol feere. Yeesu wi'iɓe, “Taarteemo, alonmo o dilla.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>25</sup>Jesus said to her, ‘I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live even if he dies.’ ... <sup>43</sup>When he had said this, he shouted in a loud voice, ‘Lazarus, come out!’ <sup>44</sup>The one who had died came out, his feet and hands tied up with strips of cloth, and a cloth wrapped around his face. Jesus said to them, ‘Unwrap him and let him go.’

elements.question3: Jesus says ’I am the resurrection and the life’ — then demonstrates it. What does this tell us about His authority over death?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus does not say He can bring resurrection or that He knows the way to life — He says He is the resurrection and the life. It is an identity claim, not just a capability claim. He then immediately backs it up by calling a man who had been dead for four days out of his tomb with a single command. Death had no power to resist Him. This tells us that Jesus’ authority over death is not borrowed or limited — it is intrinsic to who He is. And because He is the resurrection and the life, the promise He makes to believers — that they will live even if they die — rests on His own identity and power.
elements.questionHint: Notice that Jesus says ’I am the resurrection’ rather than ’I can perform resurrections’ — what is the difference between those two statements, and what does it suggest about where the power over death actually resides?

elements.question: What do you think of when you hear Jesus described as ’the Son of God’? Think about it before tapping ’Find out’.
elements.answer: People sometimes assume this means Jesus is in some way less than God — a divine being, perhaps, but not fully God Himself. The Bible, however, uses ’Son of God’ to mean the opposite. When Jesus claimed to be the Son of God, his listeners understood it as a claim to be equal with God (John 5:18). ’Son of’ in this context conveys nature and identity, not origin or rank. Jesus is fully God — not a lesser divine being, and not a created figure who became divine.

elements.text1: Shin, Yesu ya fuskanci jarabawa? Shin ya yi zunubi?
elements.text3: Was Jesus tempted? Did He sin?

elements.passageText1: <sup>15</sup>Gama Babban Firist da muke da shi ba marar juyayin kasawarmu ba ne, a'a, shi ne wanda aka jarabce shi ta kowace hanya da aka jarabce mu, amma bai yi zunubi ba.
elements.passageText3: <sup>15</sup>Gama babban firist da muke da shi ba marar juyayin kāsawarmu ba ne, amma muna da wanda aka gwada ta kowace hanya, kamar dai yadda akan yi mana, duk da haka bai yi zunubi ba.
elements.passageText4: <sup>15</sup>15 Mawɗo Mawɓe Ardiiɓe Diina mo ngooduɗen naa' o goɗɗo mo waawataa yurminanki'en enen tampuɓe. Ammaa o foondaama e koo ngole laawol no ɗum foondirta'en, fuu e non o waɗaayi hakke.
elements.passageText5: <sup>15</sup>For we do not have a high priest incapable of sympathizing with our weaknesses, but one who has been tempted in every way just as we are, yet without sin.

elements.question3: According to this verse, how does Jesus’ experience of temptation affect His ability to help us when we are tempted?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The writer says that because Jesus was tempted in every way just as we are, He is able to sympathise with our weaknesses — not from a distance, but from personal experience. He is not a high priest who is immune to the pressures we face; He knows what it is to feel the pull of temptation from the inside. This means that when we come to Him in our struggle, we are not coming to someone who shrugs and says ’I don’t understand what that feels like.’ We are coming to one who does understand, and who — crucially — came through it without sin, which means He knows the way through.
elements.questionHint: Think about whether you would rather seek help from someone who has only read about a difficult experience or from someone who has actually been through it themselves — how does that apply to what this verse is saying about Jesus?

elements.text3: To think about

elements.question1: Tayaya ka/ki iya ƙoƙarin gamsar da ɗan'uwa ko aboki cewar Yesu ya wuce a ce da shi malami nagari kawai
elements.question3: How would you try to convince your friends that Jesus was more than a good teacher?

elements.question1: Idan aka tambaye ka/ki, 'Yaya kamanin Yesu yake?' Ya za ka/ki amsa wannan tambayar?
elements.question3: If asked the question, ‘What is Jesus like?’, how would you answer?

elements.text1: Ruhu Mai Tsarki shine ya kasan na uku acikin Tirniti, ba mai 'tilastawa' bane kamar yarda mutane da yawa ke tunani. Ya kasance na uku a cikin Tirniti, kasance ɗaya da Allah Uba da Allah Ɗa (Yesu Almasihu). An kuma ƙara sanar da Ruhu Mai Tsarki a matsayin Ruhun Yesu (kuma Ruhun Almasihu da kuma Ruhun Ɗan Allah).
elements.text3: The Holy Spirit is perhaps the least understood member of the Trinity. Many people think of Him as an impersonal force or a vague spiritual feeling. But the Bible is clear: He is a person — the third person of the Trinity, alongside God the Father and God the Son. The Holy Spirit is also referred to in the Bible as the Spirit of Jesus (and of Christ and of God’s Son).

elements.text1: Tayaya mu ka san cewar Ruhun Mai Tsarki na ɗaya daga cikin Tirniti
elements.text3: How do we know that the Holy Spirit is a person?

elements.passageText1: <sup>26</sup>Haka kuma Ruhu yake yi mana taimako a kan gajiyawarmu, domin ba mu ma san abin da ya kamata mu roƙa ba, amma Ruhu da kansa yana yi mana roƙo da nishe-nishen da ba su hurtuwa. <sup>27</sup>Amma shi mai fayyace zukatanmu ya san abin da Ruhu yake niyya, domin bisa ga nufin Allah ne Ruhu yake yi wa tsarkaka roƙo.
elements.passageText3: <sup>26</sup>Haka nan ma, Ruhu yana taimakonmu cikin rashin ƙarfinmu. Ba mu san abin da ya kamata mu roƙa cikin addu’a ba, amma Ruhu kansa yana roƙo a madadinmu da nishe-nishen da kalmomi ba sa iya bayyanawa. <sup>27</sup>Shi kuma wanda yake binciken zukatanmu ya san halin Ruhu, domin Ruhu yana roƙo a madadin tsarkaka bisa ga nufin Allah.
elements.passageText4: <sup>26</sup>26 Non maa, Ruuhu Allah e walla'en to tampuɗen. Ngam en andaa no haani ngaɗiren do'aare, ammaa Ruuhu Allah e hoore maajum e toroo Allah ngam meeɗen carel ngel ndulluɗen ko mbi'eten en otta. <sup>27</sup>27 Nden Allah anduɗo ko woni nder ɓerɗe meeɗen, e mo andi ko Ruuhu yiɗi, ngam Ruuhu e toroo Allah ngam yimɓe maako non no o yiɗiri.
elements.passageText5: <sup>26</sup>In the same way, the Spirit helps us in our weakness, for we do not know how we should pray, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with inexpressible groanings. <sup>27</sup>And he who searches our hearts knows the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes on behalf of the saints according to God’s will.

elements.question3: What does the Spirit do in these verses? What does this tell us about His nature as a person rather than a force?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: In these verses the Spirit helps us in our weakness, intercedes for us when we do not know how to pray, and does so according to God’s will — meaning He has knowledge of both our needs and God’s purposes. These are not the actions of an impersonal force. A force does not intercede, does not have a ’mind’, and does not groan on behalf of someone. Intercession requires awareness, compassion, intention, and relationship — all marks of personhood. The passage uses the personal pronoun ’himself’, reinforcing that the Spirit is a ’he’, not an ’it’.
elements.questionHint: Think about what interceding for someone actually involves — could a wind or an electrical current do it? What does it require, and do those qualities belong to a force or a person?

elements.passageText1: <sup>30</sup>Ku kuma kula, kada fa ku ɓata wa Ruhu Mai Tsarki na Allah rai, wanda aka hatimce ku da shi, cewa ku nasa ne a ranar fansa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>30</sup>Kada kuma ku ɓata wa Ruhu Mai Tsarki na Allah rai, wanda da shi aka hatimce ku da shi don ranar fansa.
elements.passageText4: <sup>30</sup>30 Paamee, to' mettinanon Ruuhu Allah ɓernde, ngam Allah hokkii'onɗum ngam holla onon ɗum ɓe maako, nden Nyalnde Jaango o rimɗinay'on.
elements.passageText5: <sup>30</sup>And do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed for the day of redemption.

elements.question3: Paul says we can ’grieve’ the Holy Spirit. What does that word tell us about the kind of being the Holy Spirit is?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Grief is an emotional response — it requires the capacity to feel, to care, and to be affected by what others do. You cannot grieve a force of nature: you cannot grieve electricity, or gravity, or the wind. The fact that Paul warns us not to grieve the Holy Spirit tells us that the Spirit has feelings and is personally affected by how we live. This places Him firmly in the category of a person, not a power. It also implies that the Spirit has a relationship with us close enough that our behaviour genuinely matters to Him.
elements.questionHint: Can you grieve a tool, a machine, or a natural force? What kind of being must something be in order to experience grief, and what does that imply about the Holy Spirit?

elements.text1: Wanne irin kwatance aka yi amfani da su don bayani akan Ruhu Mai Tsarki?
elements.text3: What are some of the images used in the Bible to describe the Holy Spirit?

elements.passageText1:
<sup>37</sup>  #

  A ranar ƙarshe ta idin, wato babbar ranar, sai Yesu ya tsaya, ya ɗaga murya ya ce, “Duk mai jin ƙishirwa yă zo gare ni yă sha. <sup>38</sup># Eze 47.1 ;  Zak 14.8  Duk mai gaskatawa da ni, kamar yadda Nassi ya ce, ‘Daga zuciyarsa ne kogunan ruwan rai za su gudana.’ ” <sup>39</sup>To, wannan shi ne ya faɗa game da Ruhu, wanda masu gaskatawa da shi za su karɓa, domin har yanzu ba a ba da Ruhu ba, saboda ba a ɗaukaka Yesu ba tukuna.
elements.passageText3: <sup>37</sup>A rana ta ƙarshe kuma mafi girma ta Bikin, Yesu ya tsaya ya yi magana da babbar murya ya ce,  “Duk mai jin ƙishirwa, yă zo wurina yă sha. <sup>38</sup>Duk wanda ya gaskata da ni, kamar yadda Nassi ya ce, kogunan ruwa masu rai za su gudana daga cikinsa.” <sup>39</sup>Da wannan yana nufin Ruhu ke nan, wanda waɗanda suka gaskata da shi za su karɓa daga baya. Har yă zuwa wannan lokaci ba a ba da Ruhu ba tukuna, da yake ba a riga an ɗaukaka Yesu ba.
elements.passageText4: <sup>37</sup>37 Nyalnde nde humto ngon re'ata, kayre woni nyalnde mawnde nde humto ngon. E nyalnde nden Yeesu ummii, darii, wi'i e sembe, “Fuu nanayɗo ɗonka wara to am mi hokkaymo ko o yarata. # KLee 23.36. <sup>38</sup>38 Non no ɗum windiraa, ‘Koo moye kooliiɗoyam, canɗi ndiyam kokkoojam yonki waɗay ka iluki daga nder ɓernde maako.’ ” # Ijik 47.1; Jak 14.8. <sup>39</sup>39 Nde Yeesu wi'i ɗu'um, dow Ruuhu Allah o metata ɗum kooliiɓemo keɓata. E ngel ɗon carel Allah hokkaayi Ruuhu muuɗum taw, ngam Yeesu teddinaaka taw.
elements.passageText5: <sup>37</sup>On the last day of the feast, the greatest day, Jesus stood up and shouted out, ‘If anyone is thirsty, let him come to me, and <sup>38</sup>let the one who believes in me drink. Just as the scripture says, "From within him will flow rivers of living water."’ <sup>39</sup>(Now he said this about the Spirit, whom those who believed in him were going to receive, for the Spirit had not yet been given, because Jesus had not yet been glorified.)

elements.question3: Jesus uses the image of water to describe the Spirit. What does this image suggest about what the Spirit does in a person’s life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Water is essential for life — without it, living things wither and die. By describing the Spirit as living water that flows from within, Jesus suggests that the Spirit is not an optional extra for believers but a vital, life-giving presence. The image of ’rivers’ flowing from within also suggests abundance and outward movement — the Spirit is not a small, stagnant pool but a generous, overflowing source. He satisfies a deep spiritual thirst that nothing else can meet, and His work in us is not contained — it spills outward to those around us.
elements.questionHint: What happens to a person, plant, or animal that goes without water for a long time — and if the Spirit is like living water, what does that suggest about how essential He is to spiritual life?

elements.passageText1: <sup>2</sup>farat ɗaya, sai aka ji wani amo daga sama, kamar na busowar gawurtacciyar iska, ya cika duk gidan da suke zaune. <sup>3</sup>Sai waɗansu harsuna kamar na wuta suka bayyana a gare su, suna rarrabuwa, suna sassauka a kan ko wannensu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>2</sup>Ba zato ba tsammani sai aka ji motsi kamar ta babbar iska tana hurowa daga sama ta kuma cika dukan gidan da suke zaune. <sup>3</sup>Suka ga wani abin da ya yi kama da harsunan wuta, suka rarrabu suna kuma sassauka a kan kowannensu.
elements.passageText4: <sup>2</sup>2 Ɗon e ɗon ɓe nani iido ƴiwoyii daga dow bo hendu cembindu. Iido ngon heewi wuro ngo ɓe ngoni nder. <sup>3</sup>3 Sey goɗɗum nanduɗum e ɗemɗe hiite wangi. Ɗe cankitii, ɗe koɗi dow hoore koo moye maɓɓe.
elements.passageText5: <sup>2</sup>Suddenly a sound like a violent rushing wind came from heaven and filled the whole house where they were sitting. <sup>3</sup>And tongues spreading out like a fire appeared to them and came to rest on each one of them.

elements.question3: Wind and fire are the images here. What qualities do these suggest about the Holy Spirit’s arrival and work?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Wind and fire are both powerful, visible in their effects, and impossible to fully control or contain. The wind here is not a gentle breeze but a violent, rushing sound — suggesting the Spirit’s arrival was forceful, dramatic, and unmistakable. Fire is a purifying and consuming force, associated in the Old Testament with God’s presence. That tongues of fire rested on each individual suggests that the Spirit came not just to fill a room but to touch each person personally. Together, these images point to a Spirit whose coming is powerful, purifying, impossible to ignore, and deeply personal.
elements.questionHint: Think about the properties of wind and fire — they are both powerful, both impossible to fully control, and both visible through their effects rather than directly. What might those qualities be saying about how the Spirit works?

elements.text1: Ga su wa Allah ya ba Ruhu Mai Tsarki?
elements.text3: To whom does God give His Spirit?

elements.passageText1: <sup>9</sup>Amma ku kam, ba masu zaman halin mutuntaka ba ne, masu zaman Ruhu ne, in dai har Ruhun Allah yana zaune a zuciyarku. Duk wanda ba shi da Ruhun Almasihu kuwa, ba na Almasihu ba ne.
elements.passageText3: <sup>9</sup>Ku kam, ana mulkinku ba ta wurin mutuntaka ba, sai dai ta wurin Ruhu, in Ruhun Allah yana zaune a cikinku. In kuma wani ba shi da Ruhun Kiristi, shi ba na Kiristi ba ne.
elements.passageText4: <sup>9</sup>9 Ammaa onon on ngaɗataa ko ɓalli mooɗon ngiɗi. On ngaɗa ko Ruuhu Allah wi'i, to ni ɗum goonga Ruuhu Allah e jooɗii nder ɓerɗe mooɗon. Koo moye to walaa Ruuhu Almasiihu # 8.9:  Ruuhu Almasiihu e Ruuhu Allah fuu ɗum go'o. , naa' Almasiihu jeyimo.
elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>You, however, are not in the flesh but in the Spirit, if indeed the Spirit of God lives in you. Now if anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ, this person does not belong to him.

elements.question3: According to Paul, what is the mark of belonging to Christ? What does this tell us about whether the Spirit is only for some Christians?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says the mark of belonging to Christ is having the Spirit of Christ living in you. He then states the reverse just as clearly: if anyone does not have the Spirit, that person does not belong to Christ. There is no middle category — no such thing as a Christian without the Spirit. This means the Spirit is not a special gift for particularly advanced or committed Christians; He is given to every believer at the point of coming to faith. Receiving the Spirit is not a second step or a higher level of Christian experience — it is what it means to belong to Christ at all.
elements.questionHint: Paul states this as an either/or with no exceptions — you either have the Spirit or you don’t belong to Christ. What does that absolute framing tell you about whether the Spirit is available only to certain kinds of Christians?

elements.text1: Menene wasu daga cikin ayyukan Ruhu Mai Tsarki?
elements.text3: What do we receive from God through His Spirit?
