elements.question3: What does Jesus promise the Spirit will give us, and for what purpose? How does that apply to us today?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus promises that the Spirit will give us power, and He connects that power directly to a purpose: being His witnesses — starting locally and spreading outward to the ends of the earth. The power is not given for personal benefit but for mission. Today, the same promise applies to all believers. We are still called to be witnesses to Jesus in our homes, communities, cities, and across the world, and the Spirit is still the one who equips us for that. Without the Spirit’s power, effective witness is beyond us; with it, ordinary people can speak and act in ways that change lives.
elements.questionHint: Jesus links the gift of power directly to a specific task — what is that task, and does the fact that it stretches ’to the farthest parts of the earth’ suggest it was only meant for the first disciples, or for all followers of Jesus?

elements.text1: Tayaya Ruhu Mai Tsarki ke taimakon mu a matayin mu na Kiristoci?
elements.text3: How does the Holy Spirit help us as Christians?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>Duk da haka ina gaya muku gaskiya, zai fiye muku in tafi, domin in ban tafi ba, Mai Taimakon nan ba zai zo gare ku ba. In kuwa na tafi, zan aiko shi gare ku. <sup>8</sup>Sa'ad da kuwa ya zo zai faɗakar da duniya a kan zunubi, da adalci, da kuma hukunci. <sup>9</sup>Wato a kan zunubi, domin ba su gaskata da ni ba,
elements.passageText3: <sup>7</sup>Amma gaskiya nake gaya muku. Don amfaninku ne zan tafi. Sai ko na tafi, in ba haka ba Mai Taimakon nan ba zai zo muku ba. Amma in na tafi, zan aiko shi gare ku. <sup>8</sup>Sa’ad da ya zo kuwa, zai kā da duniya a kan zunubi da adalci da kuma hukunci, <sup>9</sup>a kan zunubi, domin mutane ba su gaskata da ni ba;
elements.passageText4: <sup>7</sup>7 Ammaa e mi wi'a'on goonga, ngam ɓeydaari mooɗon ndillaymi. Ngam to mi dillaayi, ɗum ɗon Ballayɗum # 16.7:  Waato Ruuhu Allah.  warantaa'on. Ammaa to mi dillii, mi liloyayɗum to mooɗon. <sup>8</sup>8 To ɗum waroyii, ɗum hollay yimɓe duuniyaaru ɓe paamaayi boɗɗum ko woni hakke, e aadilaaku Allah, e kiita maako. <sup>9</sup>9 Ɓe paamaayi ko woni hakke, ngam ɓe koolaakiyam.
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>But I tell you the truth, it is to your advantage that I am going away. For if I do not go away, the Advocate will not come to you, but if I go, I will send him to you. <sup>8</sup>And when he comes, he will prove the world wrong concerning sin and righteousness and judgment— <sup>9</sup>concerning sin, because they do not believe in me;
elements.note: Advocate — or, in other versions, Helper, Comforter or Counsellor — is referring to the Holy Spirit.

elements.question3: Jesus says it is to our ’advantage’ that He goes away so the Advocate can come. Why might the Spirit’s presence be described as an advantage?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: When Jesus was on earth, His physical presence was limited to one place at a time — only those near Him could hear Him, see Him, or be helped by Him. The Spirit, by contrast, is given to all believers everywhere simultaneously. Every Christian in every century and every corner of the world has access to the Spirit’s help, teaching, and comfort — not just those who happened to walk the roads of Galilee. The Spirit also works from within rather than from outside, shaping our hearts and minds in ways that a physical presence alongside us could not. In that sense, the Spirit’s coming genuinely is an advantage: He is more widely present, more intimately available, and more personally transformative.
elements.questionHint: When Jesus was physically present, how many people could be with Him at once — and how does the Spirit’s way of being present with believers differ from that limitation?

elements.passageText1: <sup>25</sup>“Duk wannan na faɗa muku ne tun muna tare. <sup>26</sup>Amma Mai Taimako, Ruhu Mai Tsarki, wanda Uba zai aiko da sunana, shi ne zai koya muku kome, ya kuma tuna muku duk abin da na faɗa muku.
elements.passageText3: <sup>25</sup>  “Duk wannan na faɗa ne yayinda ina tare da ku. <sup>26</sup>Amma Mai Taimako, Ruhu Mai Tsarki, wanda Uba zai aiko cikin sunana, zai koya muku kome, zai kuma tuna muku duk abin da na faɗa muku.
elements.passageText4: <sup>25</sup>25 “Mi wi'u'on fuu ɗu'um jooni nde ngondumi e mooɗon. <sup>26</sup>26 Ammaa e woodi Ballayɗum, waato Ruuhu Allah ɗum Nyaako Mo Dow liloyta nder inde am. Ɗum ekkitinay'on koo ɗume, ɗum siftoranay'on fuu ko mbiimi'on.
elements.passageText5: <sup>25</sup>’I have spoken these things to you while I am still with you. <sup>26</sup>But the Advocate, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, will teach you everything, and will cause you to remember everything I said to you.’

elements.question3: What two things does Jesus say the Holy Spirit will do for His followers? How do you experience either of these in your own life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus says the Holy Spirit will teach His followers everything, and will cause them to remember what Jesus said. These are two distinct but related gifts: ongoing teaching that opens up understanding of God’s truth, and a Spirit-aided memory that brings Jesus’ words back to mind when they are needed. In practice, many Christians experience this when reading Scripture and finding that a familiar passage suddenly becomes alive and meaningful in a new way, or when a verse comes to mind at exactly the moment it is needed. Both gifts point to the Spirit as the one who makes God’s word personally real and applicable in daily life.
elements.questionHint: Jesus mentions two separate things the Spirit will do — can you identify them both in verse 26, and think of a time when you may have experienced either of them while reading the Bible or facing a difficult situation?

elements.passageText1: <sup>10</sup>mu ne Allah ya bayyana wa, ta wurin Ruhu, domin Ruhu shi yake fayyace kome, har ma zurfafan al'amuran Allah. <sup>11</sup>Wane ne a cikin mutane ya san tunanin wani mutum, in ba ruhun shi mutumin ba? Haka kuma ba wanda ya san tunanin Allah, sai dai Ruhun Allah. <sup>12</sup>Mu kuwa ba ruhun duniya muka samu ba, sai dai Ruhu wanda yake daga wurin Allah, domin mu fahimci abubuwan da Allah ya yi mana baiwa hannu sake. <sup>13</sup>Su ne kuwa muke sanarwa ta maganar da ba hikimar ɗan adam ce ta koyar ba, sai dai wadda Ruhu ya koyar, muna bayyana al'amura masu ruhu ga waɗanda suke na ruhu. <sup>14</sup>Mutumin da ba shi da Ruhu yakan ƙi yin na'am da al'amuran Ruhun Allah, don wauta ne a gare shi, ba kuwa zai iya fahimtarsu ba, domin ta wurin ruhu ne ake rarrabewa da su. <sup>15</sup>Mutumin da yake na ruhu kuwa yakan rarrabe da kome, shi kansa kuma ba mai jarraba shi.
elements.passageText3: <sup>10</sup>Amma Allah ya bayyana mana shi ta wurin Ruhunsa. <sup>11</sup>Wane ne a cikin mutane ya san tunanin mutum, in ba ruhun mutumin da yake cikinsa ba? Haka kuma ba wanda ya san tunanin Allah, sai dai Ruhun Allah. <sup>12</sup>Ba mu karɓi ruhun duniya ba, sai dai Ruhun da yake daga Allah, domin mu fahimci abin da Allah ya ba mu hannu sake. <sup>13</sup>Wannan shi ne abin da muke sanarwa, ba da kalmomin da muka koya ta wurin hikimar mutum ba, amma da kalmomin da Ruhu yake koyarwa, muna faɗin gaskiya ta ruhaniya da kalmomin ruhaniya. <sup>14</sup>Mutumin da ba shi da Ruhu, ba ya yarda da abubuwan da suka fita daga Ruhun Allah, domin wauta ce a gare shi. Ba kuwa zai fahimce su ba, domin ta Ruhu ne ake fahimtarsu. <sup>15</sup>Mutumin ruhaniya yakan gwada dukan abubuwa, amma shi kansa ba ya ƙarƙashin shari’ar mutum.
elements.passageText4: <sup>10</sup>10 Ammaa Allah hollii'enɗum laawol Ruuhu maako. Ngam Ruuhu maako e andi koo ɗume haa e sirru maako lugguɗum. <sup>11</sup>11 Moye nder yimɓe andata ko woni nder ɓernde neɗɗo to naa' neɗɗo on? Non maa, walaa anduɗo nyumo Allah sey ni Ruuhu Allah. <sup>12</sup>12 Ruuhu ɗum keɓuɗen, naa' to duuniyaaru ɗum ƴiwoyi; to Allah ɗum ƴiwoyi, ngam anden caahu ɗum o hokki'en. <sup>13</sup>13 E min meta dow ɗu'um naa' nder haala ka faamu neɗɗo ekkitata, sey ni nder haala ka Ruuhu Allah ekkitinimin. E min ekkitina gooduɓe Ruuhu Allah dow goonga Allah. <sup>14</sup>14 Neɗɗo mo walaa Ruuhu Allah, o jaɓataa ko ƴiwoyi to Ruuhu Allah, ngam to maako ɗum haaŋoore. O waawataa o faamaɗum ngam o walaa Ruuhu Allah. <sup>15</sup>15 Gooduɗo Ruuhu Allah boo waaway faamuki koo ɗume ko Ruuhu Allah ekkitata. Ammaa mo walaa Ruuhu Allah faamataa nyumo gooduɗo Ruuhu Allah.
elements.passageText5: <sup>10</sup>God has revealed these things to us by the Spirit, for the Spirit searches all things, even the deep things of God. <sup>11</sup>For who among men knows the things of a man except the man’s spirit within him? So too, no one knows the things of God except the Spirit of God. <sup>12</sup>Now we have not received the spirit of the world, but the Spirit who is from God, so that we may know the things that are freely given to us by God. <sup>13</sup>And we speak about these things, not with words taught us by human wisdom, but with those taught by the Spirit, explaining spiritual things to spiritual people. <sup>14</sup>Now the unbeliever does not receive the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him. And he cannot understand them, because they are spiritually discerned. <sup>15</sup>The one who is spiritual discerns all things, yet he himself is understood by no one.

elements.question3: According to Paul, what unique role does the Spirit play in helping us understand God and His word?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul argues that just as only a person’s own spirit truly knows what is going on inside them, only God’s Spirit truly knows the deep things of God. This means that genuine understanding of God and His word is not achievable through human intelligence or study alone — it requires the Spirit’s illumination. The Spirit, who knows God from the inside, reveals these things to believers. This is why Paul says the unbeliever finds spiritual things foolish: without the Spirit, they simply cannot be discerned. For the Christian, the Spirit is the one who makes God’s truth comprehensible, personal, and transformative — turning words on a page into living revelation.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses an analogy about knowing another person’s inner thoughts — only their own spirit has access to that. How does he apply that same logic to the question of who can truly understand the things of God?

elements.passageText1: <sup>26</sup>Haka kuma Ruhu yake yi mana taimako a kan gajiyawarmu, domin ba mu ma san abin da ya kamata mu roƙa ba, amma Ruhu da kansa yana yi mana roƙo da nishe-nishen da ba su hurtuwa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>26</sup>Haka nan ma, Ruhu yana taimakonmu cikin rashin ƙarfinmu. Ba mu san abin da ya kamata mu roƙa cikin addu’a ba, amma Ruhu kansa yana roƙo a madadinmu da nishe-nishen da kalmomi ba sa iya bayyanawa.
elements.passageText4: <sup>26</sup>26 Non maa, Ruuhu Allah e walla'en to tampuɗen. Ngam en andaa no haani ngaɗiren do'aare, ammaa Ruuhu Allah e hoore maajum e toroo Allah ngam meeɗen carel ngel ndulluɗen ko mbi'eten en otta.
elements.passageText5: <sup>26</sup>In the same way, the Spirit helps us in our weakness, for we do not know how we should pray, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with inexpressible groanings.

elements.question3: Have you ever struggled to know how or what to pray? What comfort does this verse offer?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Most Christians experience times when they feel too overwhelmed, confused, or exhausted to pray — or simply do not know what to ask for. This verse offers real comfort by telling us that in precisely those moments, we are not left alone. The Spirit Himself steps in and intercedes for us, bringing before God what we cannot find words for. The phrase ’inexpressible groanings’ captures those moments of deep need that go beyond language. The comfort is not that prayer becomes easier, but that our inadequacy in prayer is covered — the Spirit carries what we cannot carry, and God, who searches our hearts, understands perfectly what the Spirit is expressing on our behalf.
elements.questionHint: Paul doesn’t say the Spirit helps us find better words — he says the Spirit intercedes when we have no words at all. What does it mean for your prayer life to know that your weakness in prayer is something the Spirit is already covering?

elements.text1: Mu karɓi kyautar baye-baye wurin Allah ta wurin Ruhu Mai Tsarki
elements.text3: What are the gifts of the Spirit?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Wani an yi masa baiwa da koyar da hikima ta wurin Ruhu, wani kuma da koyar da sani ta wurin wannan Ruhu. <sup>9</sup>Wani kuwa an yi masa baiwa da bangaskiya ta wurin Ruhun nan, wani kuma baiwar warkarwa ta wurin Ruhun nan, <sup>10</sup>wani kuma yin mu'ujizai, wani kuma annabci, wani kuma baiwar rarrabe ruhu da aljani, wani kuma iya harshe iri iri, wani kuma fassara harsuna.
elements.passageText3: <sup>8</sup>Ga wani, an ba shi saƙon hikima ta wurin Ruhu, ga wani baiwar saƙon sani ta wurin wannan Ruhu guda. <sup>9</sup>Ga wani bangaskiya ta wurin wannan Ruhu, ga wani kuma baiwar warkarwa ta wurin wannan Ruhu guda. <sup>10</sup>Ga wani yin ayyukan banmamaki, ga wani annabci, ga wani baiwar rarrabe ruhohi, ga wani baiwar magana da harsuna dabam-dabam, ga wani har wa yau iya fassarar harsuna.
elements.passageText4: <sup>8</sup>8 Ruuhu ɗum, kanjum hokkata goɗɗo dokkal wolde faamu, goɗɗo boo dokkal wolde andal. <sup>9</sup>9 Kanjum hokkata goɗɗo dokkal hoolaaki Allah, nden goɗɗo boo dokkal yamɗitinki nyawɓe, <sup>10</sup>10 goɗɗo kadin dokkal waɗuki fii kayɗiniiɗum, goɗɗo waɗuki annabaaku, goɗɗo boo mbawka feerootirki hakkunde Ruuhu Allah e ginni, goɗɗo boo mbawka waɗirki do'aare ɗemɗe ɗe ɓe andaa, goɗɗo boo mbawka hokkuki ma'ana ɗemɗe ɗen.
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>For to one is given a message of wisdom through the Spirit, and to another a message of knowledge by the same Spirit, <sup>9</sup>to another faith by the same Spirit, and to another gifts of healing by the one Spirit, <sup>10</sup>and to another performance of miracles, to another prophecy, and to another discernment of spirits, to another different kinds of tongues, and to another the interpretation of tongues.

elements.question3: List the gifts Paul mentions here. Are any of them surprising to you?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul lists: a message of wisdom, a message of knowledge, faith, gifts of healing, performance of miracles, prophecy, discernment of spirits, different kinds of tongues, and interpretation of tongues. The list spans both the seemingly ordinary (wisdom, knowledge, faith) and the more dramatic (miracles, tongues, healing). Many people are surprised to see ’faith’ listed as a gift — we might assume everyone has it equally — but Paul suggests that some are given an unusually strong or specific gift of faith. Others find the more supernatural gifts surprising, either because they did not expect them in the Bible, or because they had assumed they were no longer given today.
elements.questionHint: Look back through the list carefully — which of these gifts would you expect to find in any church today, and are there any that seem more unusual or unexpected to you, and why?

elements.passageText1: <sup>22</sup>Albarkar Ruhu kuwa ita ce ƙauna, da farin ciki, da salama, da haƙuri, da kirki, da nagarta, da aminci, <sup>23</sup>da tawali'u da kuma kamunkai. Masu yin irin waɗannan abubuwa, ba dama shari'a ta kama su. <sup>24</sup>Waɗanda kuwa suke su na Almasihu Yesu ne, sun gicciye halin mutuntaka da mugayen sha'awace-sha'awace iri iri. <sup>25</sup>In dai rayuwar tamu ta Ruhu ce, to, sai mu tafiyar da al'amuranmu ta wurin ikon Ruhu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>22</sup>Amma amfanin da Ruhu yake bayar shi ne ƙauna, farin ciki, salama, haƙuri, kirki, nagarta, aminci, <sup>23</sup>sauƙinkai, da kuma kamunkai. Ai, ba ruwan Doka da waɗannan abubuwa. <sup>24</sup>Waɗanda suke na Kiristi Yesu, sun gicciye mutuntaka tare da abubuwan da mutum yake marmari da kuma sha’awace-sha’awacensa. <sup>25</sup>Da yake muna rayuwa ta Ruhu ne, bari mu ci gaba da al’amuranmu ta wurin Ruhu.
elements.passageText4: <sup>22</sup>22 Ammaa Ruuhu Allah e wadda yidde, e welwelo, e jonde jam, e munyal, e yurmeende, e ngikku boɗɗum, e waɗuki goonga, <sup>23</sup>23 e leynuki hoore, e suruki hoore. Walaa dooka ganyuɗum kuuɗe ɗe'e. <sup>24</sup>24 Yimɓe ɓe Yeesu Almasiihu jeyi, ɓe mbarii kuuɗe kalluɗe ɗe ɓandu e fuu suunooji mayru dow gaafaangal, waato ɓe cendirii e maajum. <sup>25</sup>25 Nde Ruuhu Allah hokki'en yonki, en ngaɗu jonde no ɗum holliri'en.
elements.passageText5: <sup>22</sup>But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, <sup>23</sup>gentleness, and self-control. Against such things there is no law. <sup>24</sup>Now those who belong to Christ have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires. <sup>25</sup>If we live by the Spirit, let us also behave in accordance with the Spirit.

elements.question3: Paul calls these qualities ’fruit’ rather than ’gifts’. What does that distinction suggest about how they develop in a Christian’s life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Gifts are given instantly and can be used immediately. Fruit, by contrast, grows gradually — it requires time, the right conditions, and a living connection to the source. By calling these qualities ’fruit of the Spirit’, Paul suggests they are not switched on overnight but develop slowly and organically as we remain connected to the Spirit and walk in step with Him. Just as you cannot force fruit to ripen by willpower alone, you cannot manufacture love, patience, or self-control by trying harder — they are the natural result of the Spirit’s life growing within us over time.
elements.questionHint: Think about how fruit grows on a tree — does it appear instantly, or does it take time and depend on the health of the connection between the branch and the tree? How might that process mirror the way these qualities develop in a Christian?

elements.text1: Don Amfani su wa Ruhu Mai Tsarki ya ba da wannan baye-baye?
elements.text3: For whose benefit are the gifts given?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>An yi wa kowanne baiwa da wani buɗi na Ruhu, don kyautata wa duka. <sup>11</sup>Dukan waɗannan Ruhu ɗaya ne da yake iza su, yake kuma rarraba wa ko wannensu yadda ya nufa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>7</sup>An ba wa kowane mutum bayyanuwar Ruhu don amfanin kowa. <sup>11</sup>Duk waɗannan aiki ne na wannan Ruhu guda, yana kuma ba da su ga kowane mutum, yadda yake so.
elements.passageText4: <sup>7</sup>7 Allah e hokka koo moye neɗɗo alaama kolloojum e mo woodi Ruuhu Allah ngam ɓeydaari koo moye. <sup>11</sup>11 Fuu fii ɗu'um ɗum kuugal Ruuhu go'otum, kanjum hokkata koo moye dokkal non no ɗum suɓiri. # Room 12.6‑8.
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>To each person the manifestation of the Spirit is given for the benefit of all. ... <sup>11</sup>It is one and the same Spirit, distributing as he decides to each person, who produces all these things.

elements.question3: Who decides which gifts each person receives, and who are they ultimately for? How should that shape the way we use our gifts?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says it is the Spirit who decides which gift each person receives — it is His sovereign choice, not ours to demand or select. And verse 7 makes clear that every gift is given ’for the benefit of all’, not for personal prestige or private use. This should shape the way we use our gifts significantly: if the Spirit chose them for the community’s benefit, then hoarding them, using them for self-promotion, or refusing to use them at all is a failure to fulfil their purpose. Our gifts are entrusted to us for others — they are a form of stewardship, not ownership.
elements.questionHint: Verse 7 says gifts are given ’for the benefit of all’ — so if a gift is being used primarily to make the person look impressive or feel special, does that align with the purpose Paul describes here?

elements.text1: Menene maƙasudin ko bamban dalilin Ruhu Mai Tsarki?
elements.text3: What is the Holy Spirit’s ultimate purpose?

elements.passageText1: <sup>14</sup>Zai ɗaukaka ni, domin zai ɗauko daga cikin abin da yake nawa ya sanar da ku.
elements.passageText3: <sup>14</sup>Zai kawo ɗaukaka gare ni ta wurin ɗaukan abin da yake nawa, ya bayyana muku.
elements.passageText4: <sup>14</sup>14 Ruuhu ɗum hoccay ko mbiimi ɗum wi'a'onka, non ɗum hollirta teddungal am.
elements.passageText5: <sup>14</sup>He will glorify me, because he will receive from me what is mine and will tell it to you.

elements.question3: What does Jesus say the Spirit’s ultimate purpose is? How does this help us evaluate whether something is genuinely the Spirit’s work?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus says the Spirit’s ultimate purpose is to glorify Him — to take what belongs to Jesus and make it known. The Spirit does not draw attention to Himself or promote His own agenda; He points consistently to Jesus. This gives us a useful test for evaluating whether something is genuinely the Spirit’s work: does it exalt Jesus, or does it exalt something else — a personality, an experience, an institution? Where the Spirit is truly at work, Jesus becomes more central, more clearly known, and more deeply loved. Conversely, where the focus drifts away from Jesus, that is a signal to be cautious.
elements.questionHint: If the Spirit’s stated purpose is to glorify Jesus, what question might you ask about a teaching, experience, or movement to test whether it is genuinely the Spirit’s work?

elements.question: What comes to mind when you hear the word ’Trinity’? Think about it before tapping ’Find out’.
elements.answer: Many people find the Trinity confusing — even Christians. The word itself doesn’t appear in the Bible, which can add to the uncertainty. What the Bible does teach is that there is one God, who exists as three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. These three are distinct — they are not simply three ’modes’ or masks that God wears at different times — yet they are one God, not three. The Trinity is not a contradiction, but it is a mystery beyond full human understanding. What we can say is that the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit share the same divine nature and work together in everything God does.

elements.text3: Key truths

elements.text3: To think about

elements.question1: Acikin baye-bayen wace baiwa ce Allah ya baka? Shin ka/ki na amfani da wannan baye-baye don kyautatawa ikkilisiya? (Idan kana buƙatar domin gane baiwar ka/ki, za ka/ki iya zantawa da ɗan'uwa mai bi ko shugabanin ikkilisiya)
elements.question3: What gifts do you think God has given you? Are you using them for the good of the church? (If you need help in identifying your gifts, speak to a Christian friend or church leader.)

elements.question1: Ko akwai 'ƴaƴan Ruhu' (duba Galatiyawa 5:22-23) wanda ba ka/ki gani shaidar su a rayuwar ka/ki?
elements.question3: Are there any ’fruits of the Spirit’ (see Galatians 5:22-23) which are not evident in your life?

elements.text1: Acikin kowa ce tattaunawa da wanda ke da kokwanto cewar akwai Allah. Maganar halitta ta kan yawan tasowa. Acikin wannan binciken ba lallai ya yiwu a iya magance duka mahawara game da halitta ba amma zamu duba bayani halitta bisa ga Littafi Mai Tsarki.
elements.text3: Questions about creation come up whenever people discuss whether God exists. There is far more to explore here than one study can cover, but what matters most is what God Himself says about it. So in this study we go to the Biblical account — and it has more to say than you might expect.

elements.text1: Wanene ya kasancec cikin Halitta
elements.text3: Who was involved in the Creation?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>A sa'ad da Allah ya fara halittar Sama da duniya,
elements.passageText3: <sup>1</sup>A farko-farko, Allah ya halicci sama da ƙasa.

elements.passageText3: <sup>12</sup>Ai, ni ne wanda ya yi duniya
elements.passageText5: <sup>12</sup>I made the earth, and created mankind upon it. I—my own hands stretched out the heavens, and I gave orders to all the host of stars.

elements.question3: What do Genesis 1:1 and Isaiah 45:12 tell us about God the Father’s role in creation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Both passages present God as the sole Creator and originator of everything that exists. Genesis 1:1 states this simply and absolutely — ‘In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth’ — leaving no room for creation to have happened without Him or before Him. Isaiah 45:12 adds a note of personal directness: God speaks in the first person, saying ’I made the earth’ and ’my own hands stretched out the heavens’. He did not delegate creation to another power; He made it Himself, with full authority — even giving orders to the stars. The overall picture is of a God who is the personal, sovereign, and intentional originator of all that exists.
elements.questionHint: In Isaiah 45:12, God uses the words ’I’ and ’my own hands’ — what does that personal, direct language suggest about how involved He was in the act of creation, and whether He needed anyone else’s help?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>Tun fil'azal akwai Kalma, Kalman nan kuwa tare da Allah yake, Kalman nan kuwa Allah ne. <sup>2</sup>Shi ne tun fil'azal yake tare da Allah. <sup>3</sup>Dukan abubuwa sun kasance ta gare shi ne, ba kuma abin da ya kasance na abubuwan da suka kasance, sai ta game da shi.

elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was fully God. <sup>2</sup>The Word was with God in the beginning. <sup>3</sup>All things were created by him, and apart from him not one thing was created that has been created.

elements.passageText3: <sup>16</sup>Ta wurinsa aka halicci dukan abu, abubuwan da suke cikin sama da abubuwan da suke a duniya, waɗanda ake gani, da waɗanda ba a gani, ko kursiyoyi ko ikoki ko masu mulki ko hukumomi; an halicci dukan abu ta wurinsa da kuma dominsa ne. <sup>17</sup>Ya kasance kafin dukan abu, kuma a cikinsa ne dukan abubuwa suke a harhaɗe.
elements.passageText4: <sup>16</sup>16 Barka maako Allah tagi koo ɗume gonɗum dow e ley, ko yi'etee e ko yi'ataake, hawti e malaa'ika'en, e ginni, e koo ɗume gooduɗum laamu e bawɗe. Koo ɗume kanko tagiɗum, nden ngam maako koo ɗume tagaa. <sup>17</sup>17 Almasiihu e ɗon ko koo ɗume tagee, barka maako koo ɗume e yaha yeeso.
elements.passageText5: <sup>16</sup>For by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities—all things were created through him and for him. <sup>17</sup>And he is before all things, and in him all things hold together.

elements.question3: What do John 1:1-3 and Colossians 1:16-17 reveal about Jesus’ role in creation? Does this change how you think about Him?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: John 1:1-3 tells us that nothing was created apart from Jesus — He was not a created being but the one through whom all creation came into existence. Colossians 1:16-17 deepens this further: all things were created not just through Him but by Him and for Him — meaning the entire created order has Jesus as both its origin and its goal. Verse 17 then adds that He is ’before all things’ and that in Him all things ’hold together’, meaning the universe continues to exist and cohere because of His ongoing sustaining power. For many people, thinking of Jesus primarily as a teacher or healer, this picture of Him as the Creator and Sustainer of all things significantly changes how they understand who He is.
elements.questionHint: Colossians 1:16 says everything was created not just ’through’ Jesus but also ’for’ him — what does it mean for creation to have been made for Jesus, and how does that compare with the way you normally think about Him?

elements.passageText1: <sup>2</sup>duniya ba ta da siffa, sarari ce kawai, duhu kuwa yana lulluɓe da fuskar zurfin teku, Ruhun Allah kuma yana shawagi bisa ruwayen.
elements.passageText3: <sup>2</sup>To, ƙasa dai ba ta da siffa, babu kuma kome a cikinta, duhu ne kawai ya rufe ko’ina, Ruhun Allah kuwa yana yawo a kan ruwan.
elements.passageText5: <sup>2</sup>Now the earth was without shape and empty, and darkness was over the surface of the watery deep, but the Spirit of God was moving over the surface of the water.

elements.passageText1: <sup>30</sup>Amma sa'ad da ka hura musu numfashi, sai su rayu,
elements.passageText3: <sup>30</sup>Sa’ad da ka aika da Ruhunka,
elements.passageText5: <sup>30</sup>When you send up your breath, they are created, and you replenish the surface of the ground.

elements.question3: What do Genesis 1:2 and Psalm 104:30 reveal about the Holy Spirit’s role in creation? What does it mean that all three persons of the Trinity were involved?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Genesis 1:2 places the Spirit of God at the very beginning — moving over the formless, empty waters before God speaks creation into order. The image suggests a hovering, active presence, ready and involved from the outset. Psalm 104:30 shows that the Spirit’s creative work is not limited to the initial act: when God sends His breath (Spirit), living creatures are created and the ground is renewed. The Spirit is involved in ongoing creation and replenishment, not just in the original event. The involvement of all three persons of the Trinity — Father, Son, and Spirit — in creation tells us that creation was not the work of one member acting alone, but a unified act of the whole Godhead. The world did not come from one dimension of God but from the fullness of who God is.
elements.questionHint: Genesis 1:2 shows the Spirit present at the very start, before anything was formed — and Psalm 104:30 shows Him involved in bringing new life into being. What do those two moments together suggest about the scope of the Spirit’s involvement in creation?

elements.text1: Menene aka halitta?
elements.text3: What did God create?

elements.question: It’s worth reading the whole passage before answering the question below…
elements.answer:
1In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth. 2Now the earth was without shape and empty, and darkness was over the surface of the watery deep, but the Spirit of God was moving over the surface of the water. 3God said, "Let there be light." And there was light! 4God saw that the light was good, so God separated the light from the darkness. 5God called the light "day" and the darkness "night." There was evening, and there was morning, marking the first day.

6God said, "Let there be an expanse in the midst of the waters and let it separate water from water." 7So God made the expanse and separated the water under the expanse from the water above it. It was so. 8God called the expanse "sky." There was evening, and there was morning, a second day.

9God said, "Let the water under the sky be gathered to one place and let dry ground appear." It was so. 10God called the dry ground "land" and the gathered waters he called "seas." God saw that it was good. 11God said, "Let the land produce vegetation: plants yielding seeds and trees on the land bearing fruit with seed in it, according to their kinds." It was so. 12The land produced vegetation—plants yielding seeds according to their kinds, and trees bearing fruit with seed in it according to their kinds. God saw that it was good. 13There was evening, and there was morning, a third day.

14God said, "Let there be lights in the expanse of the sky to separate the day from the night, and let them be signs to indicate seasons and days and years, 15and let them serve as lights in the expanse of the sky to give light on the earth." It was so. 16God made two great lights—the greater light to rule over the day and the lesser light to rule over the night. He made the stars also. 17God placed the lights in the expanse of the sky to shine on the earth, 18to preside over the day and the night, and to separate the light from the darkness. God saw that it was good. 19There was evening, and there was morning, a fourth day.

20God said, "Let the water swarm with swarms of living creatures and let birds fly above the earth across the expanse of the sky." 21God created the great sea creatures and every living and moving thing with which the water swarmed, according to their kinds, and every winged bird according to its kind. God saw that it was good. 22God blessed them and said, "Be fruitful and multiply and fill the water in the seas, and let the birds multiply on the earth." 23There was evening, and there was morning, a fifth day.

24God said, "Let the land produce living creatures according to their kinds: cattle, creeping things, and wild animals, each according to their kind." It was so. 25God made the wild animals according to their kinds, the cattle according to their kinds, and all the creatures that creep along the ground according to their kinds. God saw that it was good.

26Then God said, "Let us make humankind in our image, after our likeness, so they may rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air, over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over all the creatures that move on the earth." 27God created humankind in his own image, in the image of God he created them, male and female he created them.

28God blessed them and said to them, "Be fruitful and multiply! Fill the earth and subdue it! Rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air and every creature that moves on the ground."

31God saw all that he had made—and it was very good! There was evening, and there was morning, the sixth day.

2:1The heavens and the earth were completed with everything that was in them. 2By the seventh day God finished the work that he had been doing, and he ceased on the seventh day all the work that he had been doing. 3God blessed the seventh day and made it holy because on it he ceased all the work that he had been doing in creation.


(NET - see context ↗)

elements.question3: From Genesis 1:1–2:3, list six main things God created. What pattern or structure do you notice across the six days?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Six main things God created: (1) light and the separation of day and night; (2) the sky and the separation of waters; (3) dry land, seas, and vegetation; (4) the sun, moon, and stars; (5) sea creatures and birds; (6) land animals and human beings. A clear pattern emerges across the six days: the first three days form the environments or ’spaces’ (light, sky, land/sea), and the next three days fill those spaces with their corresponding inhabitants (luminaries fill the sky, fish and birds fill sea and sky, animals and humans fill the land). Each day ends with ’God saw that it was good’, and the whole sequence concludes with ’very good’. The pattern moves from formless and empty to ordered and filled — showing creation as deliberate, structured, and purposeful.
elements.questionHint: Look at days 1-3 and then days 4-6 side by side — do you notice a relationship between what is created on day 1 and day 4, day 2 and day 5, day 3 and day 6? What does that pairing suggest about the structure of the account?

elements.text1: Tayaya Allah ya halicci duniya?
elements.text3: How did God create the world?

elements.passageText3: <sup>3</sup>Sai Allah ya ce, “Bari haske yă kasance,” sai kuwa ga haske.
elements.passageText5: <sup>3</sup>God said, ‘Let there be light,‘ and there was light.
elements.note: See also verses 6, 9, 11 and others in the same chapter.

elements.question3: God creates simply by speaking. What does this tell us about His power and authority over the created world?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The repeated pattern of ’God said... and it was so’ throughout Genesis 1 shows that God’s word alone is sufficient to bring anything into existence. He does not need materials, effort, tools, or assistance — He simply speaks and reality responds instantly and completely. This tells us that God’s authority over creation is absolute: the created world does not exist independently of Him but is entirely subject to His command. No human being, no force of nature, and nothing in the universe has power comparable to God’s — creation came into being by His word, continues to exist by His sustaining power, and answers to Him entirely.
elements.questionHint: When a human being gives an order, it might or might not be obeyed — but when God speaks in Genesis 1, what always happens immediately, and what does that instant, total response tell you about the kind of authority He has?

elements.text1: Menene Allah ya yi tunani bayan ya gama hallatar komai
elements.text3: What did God think about everything He had created?

elements.passageText1: <sup>31</sup>Allah kuwa ya ga dukan abin da ya yi, ga shi kuwa yana da kyau ƙwarai. Ga maraice, ga safiya, kwana na shida ke nan.
elements.passageText3: <sup>31</sup>Allah ya ga dukan abin da ya yi, yana da kyau ƙwarai. Yamma ta kasance, safiya kuma ta kasance, kwana ta shida ke nan.
elements.passageText5: <sup>31</sup>God saw all that he had made—and it was very good! There was evening, and there was morning, the sixth day.

elements.question3: God declares His creation ’very good’. What does that tell us about the world as God originally made it — and why does that matter for how we think about the world now?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The declaration ’very good’ tells us that the world as God originally made it was not flawed, broken, or morally neutral — it was perfectly aligned with God’s intention and reflected His character. There was no suffering, no death, no corruption. This matters enormously for how we think about the world now, because it means the pain, brokenness, and evil we see are not original features of creation — they are intrusions. The world as it now is has been damaged, not designed that way. This gives us reason to grieve what has been lost and hope for what God has promised to restore — it also means we cannot blame God for the state of the world as it currently exists.
elements.questionHint: If God called the original creation ’very good’ but the world we see today contains suffering and brokenness, what does that difference suggest about whether God designed things this way, or whether something has gone wrong since?

elements.text1: Menene dalilin da ya sa Allah halacci ƴan adam
elements.text3: Why did God create the world?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>Dubi yadda sararin sama yake bayyana ɗaukakar Allah!
elements.passageText3: <sup>1</sup>Sammai suna shelar ɗaukakar Allah;
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>The heavens declare the glory of God; the sky displays his handiwork.

elements.question3: According to this verse, what purpose does creation serve? Can you think of a moment when the natural world has pointed you towards God?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: According to this verse, creation serves the purpose of declaring God’s glory and displaying His handiwork. The heavens are not silent witnesses — they actively proclaim something about their Maker. Creation is, in a sense, a constant, universal testimony to God’s existence, power, and beauty, available to every person in every age and culture. Many people find that moments in the natural world — a vast night sky, a mountain range, a coastline, the intricacy of a living creature — produce a sense of awe that points instinctively beyond the thing itself to the one who made it. This is precisely what the psalmist says creation is designed to do.
elements.questionHint: The psalm says the heavens ’declare’ something — as if creation is actively communicating a message. What is the message it is declaring, and have you ever found yourself sensing that message when looking at something in the natural world?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>Su mutanena ne, na kaina,
elements.passageText3: <sup>7</sup>kowa da ake kira da sunana,
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>everyone who is called by my name, whom I created for my glory, whom I formed and made.

elements.question3: Isaiah says God formed and made us for His glory. What does it mean to live for God’s glory in practice?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: To live for God’s glory means to live in a way that makes God look great — that draws attention to His character, His goodness, and His worth rather than to ourselves. In practice, this is not about a single dramatic act but about the orientation of an entire life: the choices we make, the way we treat people, the things we pursue and prioritise, and whether our lives point others towards God or away from Him. Since we are made in God’s image (Genesis 1:27), living for His glory also means reflecting His character — His love, justice, faithfulness, and compassion — in our everyday lives. The purpose of our existence, according to Isaiah, is not self-fulfilment but God-centred living.
elements.questionHint: If being created ’for God’s glory’ means our lives are meant to point to Him, what kinds of things might a person do — or avoid doing — in ordinary daily life that would either reflect or contradict that purpose?

elements.text1: Menene ya sa muka zama da mahimmanci?
elements.text3: Why did God create human beings?

elements.passageText1: <sup>27</sup># Far 5.1 , 2 # Mat 19.4 ;  Mar 10.6  Haka nan fa, Allah ya halicci mutum cikin siffarsa, cikin siffar Allah, Allah ya halicci mutum, namiji da ta mace ya halicce su.
elements.passageText3: <sup>27</sup>Saboda haka Allah ya halicci mutum cikin siffarsa.
elements.passageText5: <sup>27</sup>God created mankind in his own image, in the image of God he created them, male and female he created them.

elements.question3: What does it mean that human beings are made ’in the image of God’? How does this set us apart from the rest of creation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Being made in the image of God means that human beings uniquely reflect something of God’s own nature — His rationality, His moral sense, His capacity for relationship, creativity, and love. No other creature in Genesis is described this way. The animals are made ’according to their kinds’, but human beings are made according to God’s own image and likeness, which places us in an entirely different category. It means we are not merely biological organisms but personal beings designed for relationship with God, capable of knowing Him, representing Him in the world, and being held morally accountable to Him. It also means that every human life has an inherent dignity that cannot be stripped away, because it is God’s image that is borne in each person.
elements.questionHint: Genesis describes animals being made ’according to their kinds’ but describes humans differently — made in the image and likeness of God Himself. What does that different description suggest about the kind of relationship God intends to have with human beings compared to the rest of creation?

elements.text1: An halicce mu acikin kamannin Allah. Haka nan fa, Allah ya halicci mutum cikin siffarsa, cikin siffar Allah, Allah ya halicci mutum, namiji da ta mace ya halicce su. Farawa 1:27
elements.text3: The fact that we are made in God’s image sets human beings apart from the rest of creation. It means we are not simply the product of chance or biology — we are creatures made for relationship with God, capable of reflecting His character in the way we live.

elements.text1: Wane irin aiki Allah ya ba ƴan adam?
elements.text3: What responsibility has God given human beings?

elements.question3: What two responsibilities do these verses give to human beings in relation to God’s creation? How well do you think we are fulfilling them today?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The two responsibilities are: (1) to rule over creation — exercising authority and oversight on God’s behalf as His representatives in the world; and (2) to care for and maintain it — the language of Genesis 2:15 is that of a gardener or steward, tending and preserving what has been entrusted. These are complementary, not contradictory: the authority to rule is given in the context of responsible care, not exploitation. As for how well we are fulfilling these responsibilities today — many would argue honestly that the widespread environmental damage, species loss, and exploitation of natural resources reflects a failure of the stewardship God intended. The mandate to rule was never a licence to destroy; it was a call to serve creation on God’s behalf.
elements.questionHint: Genesis 1:26 gives humans authority to ’rule over’ creation, while Genesis 2:15 says the man was placed in the garden to ’care for it and maintain it’ — do those two ideas feel like the same thing to you, or do they add different dimensions to what responsible stewardship looks like?

elements.text3: In the last study we saw that the world God made was very good — perfect. So what went wrong? In this study we look at sin: where it came from, what it does to us, and how God responds to it.

elements.question3: What picture does the writer of Ecclesiastes paint of life under the sun? What is your reaction to his description?
elements.sampleAnswer: The writer paints a picture of life as fundamentally empty and futile — all human activity and striving amounts to ’chasing after wind’, which is a vivid image for pursuing something that can never be caught or held. Verse 15 deepens the bleakness: what is bent cannot be straightened, and what is missing cannot even be fully counted. In other words, the world is broken in ways that human effort cannot fix and human wisdom cannot fully diagnose. Many people find this description uncomfortably honest — it matches the nagging sense that however much we achieve, something remains deeply unsatisfying about life lived without reference to God.
elements.questionHint: The phrase ’chasing after wind’ describes trying to catch something — what is the point the writer is making about whether that chase can ever succeed, and do you think that rings true of how people experience life?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>Ba dama duniya ta ƙi ku, amma ni take ƙi, don na shaide ta a kan ayyukanta mugaye ne.
elements.passageText3: <sup>7</sup>Duniya ba za tă iya ƙinku ba, amma ni take ƙi domin ina shaida cewa ayyukanta mugaye ne.
elements.passageText4: <sup>7</sup>7 Jooni duuniyaaru heɓaayi ko wanyirta'on, ammaa e ndu wanyiiyam ngam e mi wi'a kuuɗe mayru ɗe kalluɗe.
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>The world cannot hate you, but it hates me, because I testify about it that its deeds are evil.

elements.question3: What does Jesus say about the deeds of the world? Why do you think the world reacted to him with hatred?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus says the world’s deeds are evil, and that this honest testimony is precisely the reason the world hates Him. He is not speaking as someone who hates the world, but as one who tells the truth about it — and the truth is unwelcome. The hatred is a reaction to exposure: people who are comfortable with their way of living do not want someone to name it as evil. This is a pattern we recognise: we tend to dislike people who hold a mirror up to behaviour we would rather not examine. The world’s hostility to Jesus is therefore itself a kind of evidence that His assessment is accurate.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says the world hates Him specifically because He testifies about its deeds — why might telling the truth about someone’s behaviour cause them to react with hostility rather than gratitude?

elements.passageText1: <sup>19</sup>Ga shi, duk halitta tana marmari, tana ɗokin ganin bayyanar 'ya'yan Allah. <sup>20</sup># Far 3.17-19  An sarayar da halitta ta zama banza, ba da nufinta ba, amma da nufin wanda ya sarayar da ita. Duk da haka, akwai sa zuciya, <sup>21</sup>gama za a 'yantar da halitta da kanta ma, daga bautar ruɓewa, domin ta sami 'yancin nan, na ɗaukaka na 'ya'yan Allah. <sup>22</sup>Mun dai san dukan halitta tana nishi, na shan azaba kuma irin ta mai naƙuda, har ya zuwa yanzu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>19</sup>Halitta tana jira da marmari ta ƙosa ta ga bayyanuwar ’ya’yan Allah. <sup>20</sup>Gama an sa halitta a cikin wulaƙanci, ba da son ta ba, sai dai ta wurin nufin wannan da ya mai da ita haka, da bege <sup>21</sup>cewa za a ’yantar da halitta kanta daga bautarta ga ruɓewa a kuma kawo ta cikin ’yancin ɗaukakar ’ya’yan Allah. <sup>22</sup>Mun san cewa dukan halitta tana nishi sai ka ce mace mai naƙuda har yă zuwa wannan lokaci.
elements.passageText4: <sup>19</sup>19 Fuu ko tagaa e heɗii e haaɓi ngam yi'a Allah wanginii ɓiɓɓe muuɗum. <sup>20</sup>20 Ngam ko tagaa wartiraama meere, naa' ngam yidde maajum, ammaa non Allah yiɗiri ɗum waɗoroo. Fuu e non e woodi kammunde, # Fuɗ 3.17‑19. <sup>21</sup>21 ngam ko tagaa e hoore maajum ɗum dimɗinteeɗum daga maccungaaku nyoluki, ɗum heɓa rimɗineeki e teddineeki ɗum yimɓe Allah njeyi. <sup>22</sup>22 En andi haa jooni fuu ko tagaa e otta ngam nawɗum no debbo piidoowo e nawɗum danyngol ottirta.
elements.passageText5: <sup>19</sup>For the creation eagerly waits for the revelation of the sons of God. <sup>20</sup>For the creation was subjected to futility—not willingly, but because of the one who subjected it—in hope <sup>21</sup>that the creation itself will also be set free from the bondage of decay into the glorious liberty of the children of God. <sup>22</sup>For we know that the whole creation groans and suffers together until now.

elements.question3: What has happened to creation as a result of sin? What does Paul say creation is waiting for?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says that the whole of creation has been subjected to futility and is in bondage to decay — it is not functioning as it was originally intended to. The language of groaning and suffering captures the brokenness we see in the natural world: disease, death, disaster, and deterioration. Crucially, Paul says this happened not willingly but as a consequence of human sin — creation was dragged into the Fall. However, the passage is not without hope: creation is not just suffering, it is waiting and longing. It eagerly anticipates the day when God’s children are fully revealed and creation itself is set free from decay into a new kind of glorious freedom.
elements.questionHint: Paul says creation was subjected to futility ’not willingly’ — what does that tell you about whether the brokenness in the natural world is creation’s own fault or a consequence of something human beings did?

elements.text1: Ya Littafi Mai Tsarki ya yi bayani akan zunubi?
elements.text3: How does the Bible describe sin?

elements.passageText1:
<sup>6</sup>  # 
 1Bit 2.25 
 Dukanmu muna kama da tumakin da suka ɓata,
elements.passageText3: <sup>6</sup>Dukanmu, muna kama da tumakin da suka ɓata,
elements.passageText5: <sup>6</sup>All of us had wandered off like sheep; each of us had turned his own way. The Lord let the guilt of all of us fall on him.

elements.question3: How does Isaiah describe the nature of sin using the image of sheep? What does it mean to have ’turned his own way’?
elements.sampleAnswer: Sheep are not known for dramatic rebellion — they wander. They do not storm off defiantly; they simply drift, following whatever attracts them in the moment, unaware of how far they have strayed from the shepherd and how much danger they are in. This is a telling image for sin: much of it is not dramatic wickedness but a gradual, unremarkable drifting away from God. To have ’turned his own way’ means each person has set their own course rather than following God’s — making themselves the authority on how to live. It is self-direction rather than God-direction, which the Bible understands as the essence of sin.
elements.questionHint: Sheep don’t usually rebel dramatically — they just wander gradually without realising how lost they are becoming. How does that kind of slow drift compare to the way you might normally picture sin, and does it change your understanding of it?

elements.passageText1: <sup>43</sup>Sau da yawa Ubangiji yakan ceci jama'arsa,
elements.passageText3: <sup>43</sup>Sau da yawa ya cece su,
elements.passageText5: <sup>43</sup>Many times he delivered them, but they had a rebellious attitude and were degraded by their sin.

elements.question3: What does this verse reveal about both the persistence of human sin and the persistence of God’s response?
elements.sampleAnswer: The verse reveals that human sin is stubbornly persistent: despite being delivered ’many times’, the people returned again and again to a rebellious attitude. Sin is not merely a series of isolated bad choices — the verse describes an underlying disposition of rebellion that keeps reasserting itself. But the verse equally reveals that God’s response is just as persistent: He delivered them ’many times’, not once. He did not give up after the first failure, or the second, or the tenth. The pattern on the human side is repeated failure; the pattern on God’s side is repeated rescue. The verse also notes the degrading effect of sin — it diminishes the people, not just morally but in their whole condition.
elements.questionHint: The verse says God delivered them ’many times’ — what does the word ’many’ suggest about how patient God was with their repeated failures, and what does the pattern of repeated rescue tell you about His character?

elements.text1: Wane lokaci ne Zunubi ya fara zuwa duniya?
elements.text3: When did sin first come into the world?

elements.passageText1: <sup>16</sup>Ubangiji Allah ya yi wa mutumin umarni, ya ce, “Kana da 'yanci ka ci daga kowane itace da yake a gonar, <sup>17</sup>amma daga itacen sanin nagarta da mugunta ba za ka ci ba, gama a ranar da ka ci shi za ka mutu lalle.”
elements.passageText3: <sup>16</sup>Ubangiji  Allah ya umarci mutumin ya ce, “Kana da ’yanci ka ci daga kowane itace a cikin lambun, <sup>17</sup>amma kada ka ci daga itacen sanin abin da yake mai kyau da abin da yake mugu, gama a ranar da ka ci shi, lalle za ka mutu.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>16</sup>Then the Lord God commanded the man, ‘You may freely eat fruit from every tree of the orchard, <sup>17</sup>but you must not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, for when you eat from it you will surely die.’

elements.question3: What had God commanded, and what consequence did he attach to disobedience?
elements.sampleAnswer: God gave a generous permission — the man could freely eat from every tree of the orchard — alongside a single clear prohibition: he must not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. The command was not restrictive in any harsh sense; the one forbidden tree was set against the backdrop of abundant provision. The consequence attached to disobedience was death: ‘you will surely die.’ This was not a threat but a statement of consequence — crossing this boundary would bring death into a world where it had not previously existed. The command established that God’s word was the boundary of life, and to step outside it was to step towards death.
elements.questionHint: Before giving the one prohibition, God said the man could ’freely eat’ from every other tree — what does that generous framing tell you about the kind of God who was setting this boundary, and does that change how you read the single restriction?

elements.passageText1:
<sup>1</sup>  # 
 W.Yah 12.9 ;  20.2    Maciji ya fi kowace dabba da Ubangiji Allah ya yi wayo. Ya ce wa matar, “Ko Allah ya ce, ‘Ba za ku ci daga wani itacen da yake a gonar ba?’ ” <sup>2</sup>Sai matar ta ce wa macijin, “Mā iya ci daga cikin itatuwan gonar, <sup>3</sup>amma Allah ya ce, ‘Ba za ku ci daga cikin 'ya'yan itacen da yake tsakiyar gonar ba, ba za ku taɓa shi ba, don kada ku mutu.’ ” <sup>4</sup>Amma macijin ya ce wa matar, “Hakika ba za ku mutu ba. <sup>5</sup>Gama Allah ya sani a ranar da kuka ci daga itacen nan idanunku za su buɗe, za ku kuwa zama kamar Allah, ku san nagarta da mugunta.” <sup>6</sup>Matar ta ga yadda itacen yana da kyau, 'ya'yansa kuma kyawawa, abin sha'awa ne kuma ga ido, abin marmari ne domin ba da hikima, ta tsinka daga 'ya'yansa, ta kuwa ci, ta kuma bai wa mijinta waɗansu, shi kuma ya ci. <sup>7</sup>Sai dukansu biyu idanunsu suka buɗe, sa'an nan suka gane tsirara suke, sai suka samo ganyayen ɓaure suka ɗinɗinka suka yi wa kansu sutura.
elements.passageText3: <sup>1</sup>To, maciji dai ya fi kowane a cikin namun jejin da  Ubangiji  Allah ya yi wayo. Ya ce wa macen, “Tabbatacce ne Allah ya ce, ‘Kada ku ci daga kowane itace a lambu’?” <sup>2</sup>Macen ta ce wa macijin, “Za mu iya ci daga ’ya’ya itatuwa a lambun, <sup>3</sup>amma Allah ya ce, ‘Kada ku ci daga ’ya’yan itacen da yake tsakiyar lambu, kada kuma ku taɓa shi, in ba haka ba kuwa za ku mutu.’ ” <sup>4</sup>Maciji ya ce wa macen, “Tabbatacce ba za ku mutu ba. <sup>5</sup>Gama Allah ya san cewa sa’ad da kuka ci daga itacen idanunku za su buɗe, za ku kuma zama kamar Allah, ku san abin da yake mai kyau da abin da yake mugu.” <sup>6</sup>Sa’ad da macen ta ga ’ya’yan itacen suna da kyau don abinci, abin sha’awa ga ido, abin marmari kuma don samun hikima, sai ta tsinka ta ci, ta kuma ba da waɗansu wa mijinta wanda yake tare da ita, shi ma ya ci. <sup>7</sup>Sa’an nan idanun dukansu biyu suka buɗe, suka kuma gane tsirara suke; saboda haka suka ɗinɗinka ganyayen ɓaure suka yi wa kansu sutura.
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>Now the serpent was more shrewd than any of the wild animals the Lord God had made. He said to the woman, ‘Is it really true that God said, "You must not eat from any tree of the orchard"?’. . . <sup>6</sup>When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was desirable for imparting wisdom, she took some of its fruit and ate; and she also gave some to her husband who was with her, and he ate. <sup>7</sup>Then the eyes of both of them were opened, and they knew they were naked; so they sewed fig leaves together and made coverings for themselves.

elements.question3: What strategy did the serpent use to tempt the woman? What motivated her to eat the fruit?
elements.sampleAnswer: The serpent’s strategy was to sow doubt about God’s word — beginning with a question that subtly distorted what God had actually said (‘Did God really say you must not eat from any tree?’), and then implying that God had an ulterior motive in giving the command. The tactic moved from questioning God’s word to questioning God’s goodness. The woman’s motivations for eating the fruit were threefold: she saw it was good for food (physical appetite), a delight to the eyes (aesthetic desire), and desirable for gaining wisdom (the ambition to be more). These three motivations — appetite, beauty, and pride — represent a recognisably human pattern of temptation that has continued ever since.
elements.questionHint: The serpent’s first move was not to say ’disobey God’ but to ask a question that cast doubt on what God had said — why is making someone question the reliability of God’s word an effective first step towards getting them to disobey it?

elements.text1: Menene zunubi ke haddasa zuwan sa? Daga ina ma ya zo?
elements.text3: What does sin involve? Where does it come from?

elements.passageText1: <sup>14</sup>Sai ya sāke kiran jama'a, yă ce musu, “Ku saurare ni dukanku, ku kuma fahimta. <sup>15</sup>Ba abin da yake shiga mutum daga waje yă ƙazanta shi, sai abin da yake fita, yake ƙazanta shi. <sup>16</sup>[Duk mai kunnen ji, yă ji.]” <sup>17</sup>Bayan rabuwarsa da taron, da ya shiga gida, almajiransa suka tambaye shi ma'anar misalin. <sup>18</sup>Ya ce musu, “Ashe, ku ma ba ku da fahimta ne? Ashe, ba ku gane ba, kome ya shiga mutum daga waje, ba yadda zai ƙazanta shi, <sup>19</sup>tun da yake ba zuciya tasa ya shiga ba, cikinsa ne, ta haka kuma zai fita?” Ta haka Yesu ya halatta kowane irin abinci. <sup>20</sup>Ya kuma ce, “Abin da yake fita daga cikin mutum yake ƙazanta shi. <sup>21</sup>Don daga ciki ne, wato daga zuciyar mutum, mugayen tunani yake fitowa, kamar su fasikanci, da sata, da kisankai, da zina, <sup>22</sup>da kwaɗayi, da mugunta, da ha'inci, da fajirci, da kishi, da yanke, da girmankai, da kuma wauta. <sup>23</sup>Duk waɗannan mugayen abubuwa daga ciki suke fitowa, suna kuwa ƙazanta mutum.”
elements.passageText3: <sup>14</sup>Sai Yesu ya sāke kiran taron wurinsa ya ce,  “Ku saurare ni dukanku, ku kuma fahimci wannan. <sup>15</sup>Ba abin da yake shiga mutum daga waje ba ne yake ƙazantar da shi. Sai dai abin da ya fita daga cikin mutum ne, yake ƙazantar da shi.” # 7.15  Waɗansu rubuce-rubucen hannu na farko-farko suna da  “marar tsabta.”  16  Duk mai kunnuwan ji, yă ji <sup>17</sup>Bayan ya bar taron, ya shiga gida, sai almajiransa suka tambaye shi ma’anar wannan misali. <sup>18</sup>Ya ce,  “Ashe, ba ku da saurin ganewa? Ba ku gane cewa ba abin da yake shiga mutum daga waje ba ne yake ƙazantar da shi. <sup>19</sup>Gama ba ya shiga cikin zuciyarsa, sai dai cikin tumbinsa, sa’an nan kuma yă fita daga jikinsa.”  (Da faɗin haka, Yesu ya nuna tsabtar kowane irin abinci.) <sup>20</sup>Ya ci gaba,  “Abin da yake fitowa daga mutum ne yake ƙazantar da shi. <sup>21</sup>Gama daga ciki, daga zuciyar mutane, mugayen tunani suke fitowa, fasikanci, sata, kisankai, zina, <sup>22</sup>kwaɗayi, ƙeta, ruɗu, ƙazantaccen sha’awa, kishi, ɓata suna, girman kai da kuma wauta. <sup>23</sup>Duk waɗannan mugayen abubuwa daga cikin suke fitowa, suna kuma ƙazantar da mutum.”
elements.passageText4: <sup>14</sup>14 Sey Yeesu fuɗɗiti noddi yimɓe hawrutuɓe, o wi'i, “Kettindee, on fuu, paamon. <sup>17</sup>17 Nde o sendiri e yimɓe fuu, o natti wuro, tokkuɓemo ƴamimo ma'ana ekkitinol ngo'ol. <sup>18</sup>18 O nootii o wi'i, “Onon maa on paamaayi ma'ana ɗu'um? On andaa walaa ko nattata neɗɗo daga yaasi wartiramo mo laaɓaa? <sup>19</sup>19 Ngam naa' nder ɓernde ɗum nattata, ammaa nder reedu, nden ɗum wurto ɓandu.” (Nde Yeesu wi'i ɗu'um, o hollii koo nduye nyamndu ndu lamndu.) <sup>20</sup>20 O ɓeydi wi'uki, “Ko wurtotoo daga nder ɓernde neɗɗo, kanjum waɗata neɗɗo warta mo laaɓaa yeeso Allah. <sup>21</sup>21 Ngam daga nder ɓernde neɗɗo nyumo kallungo wurtoytoo, e waɗuki njeenu hakkunde yimɓe ɓe ngalaa teegal, e nguyka, e warhoore, e njeenu, <sup>22</sup>22 e suuno fii duuniyaaru, e halleende, e ƴoyre kallunde, e fuu kuuɗe kalluɗe ɗe ɓerɗe maɓɓe ngiɗi, e lawliiru, e wonnuki inde neɗɗo, e mawninki hoore, e wanyuki waɗuki ko wooɗi. <sup>23</sup>23 Fuu ɗe'e kuuɗe kalluɗe daga nder ɓernde ɗe mburtoytoo ɗe ngartira neɗɗo mo laaɓaa yeeso Allah.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>14</sup>Then he called the crowd again and said to them, ‘Listen to me, everyone, and understand. <sup>15</sup>There is nothing outside of a person that can defile him by going into him. Rather, it is what comes out of a person that defiles him.’ . . . <sup>21</sup>For from within, out of the human heart, come evil ideas, sexual immorality, theft, murder, <sup>22</sup>adultery, greed, evil, deceit, debauchery, envy, slander, pride, and folly. <sup>23</sup>All these evils come from within and defile a person.’

elements.question3: According to Jesus, where does sin originate? How does this challenge the idea that our environment or upbringing is primarily responsible for our behaviour?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus locates the origin of sin firmly within the human heart. Evil does not primarily come into us from outside — it comes out of us from within. This directly challenges the popular idea that people are basically good and only made bad by difficult circumstances, poor upbringing, or a corrupt environment. While those factors may shape how sin expresses itself, Jesus says they are not its source. The source is the human heart itself. This is a sobering diagnosis because it means the problem cannot be solved simply by improving conditions, education, or social structures — the root is internal, and it requires an internal solution.
elements.questionHint: Many people believe that if you could give everyone a good upbringing and a fair environment, people would naturally behave well — how does Jesus’ diagnosis in this passage agree or disagree with that assumption, and what are the implications either way?

elements.question: What comes to mind when you hear the word sin?
elements.answer: For many people, ‘sin’ brings to mind a list of particularly bad actions — murder, theft, serious crimes. By that measure, most of us feel fairly confident we are not sinners. But the Bible uses the word much more broadly. Sin is any thought, word, or action that falls short of God’s standard — and, more than that, it is an attitude of the heart that places self at the centre rather than God. It is rebellion against God’s authority over our lives, often in quiet, respectable ways. When we understand sin this way, the Bible’s claim that ’all have sinned’ (Romans 3:23) starts to make a great deal more sense.

elements.text1: Ya Allah ke ji game da zunubi?
elements.text3: How does God feel about sin?

elements.passageText1:
<sup>5</sup>  # 
 Mat 24.37 ;  Luk 17.26 ;  1Bit 3.20    Ubangiji kuwa ya ga muguntar mutum ta ƙasaita a duniya, dukan zace-zacen tunanin zuciyarsa kuma mugunta ne kullayaumin. <sup>6</sup>Ubangiji ya damu da ya yi mutum a duniya, abin ya ɓata masa zuciya ƙwarai.
elements.passageText3: <sup>5</sup>  Ubangiji  kuwa ya lura cewa muguntar mutum ta yi yawa a duniya, kuma kowane abin da yake tunani a zuciyarsa mugu ne kawai a kowanne lokaci. <sup>6</sup>Ubangiji  ya damu da ya yi mutum a duniya, abin ya zafe shi ƙwarai.
elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>But the Lord saw that the wickedness of humankind had become great on the earth. Every inclination of the thoughts of their minds was only evil all the time. <sup>6</sup>The Lord regretted that he had made humankind on the earth, and he was highly offended.

elements.question3: How does God respond emotionally to human wickedness? What does this tell us about how seriously God takes sin?
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s response is one of grief and deep offence — He regretted making humankind and was highly offended by what He saw. These are strong emotional terms that tell us sin is not a matter of indifference to God. He is not a detached observer who shrugs at human wickedness; He is personally grieved by it. This matters because it shows that sin is not primarily about breaking rules — it is about breaking relationship. God made human beings for closeness with Himself, and seeing them given over entirely to evil (‘only evil all the time’) causes Him genuine pain. The depth of His emotional response is a measure of how seriously He takes sin.
elements.questionHint: The passage says God ’regretted’ making humankind and was ’highly offended’ — what do those emotional words tell you about whether God sees sin as a minor inconvenience or something that genuinely affects Him?

elements.passageText1: <sup>18</sup>Gama ana bayyana fushin Allah daga Sama, a kan dukan rashin bin Allah, da aikin mugunta na mutanen da suke danne gaskiya, ta aikin muguntarsu,
elements.passageText3: <sup>18</sup>Ana bayyana fushin Allah daga sama a kan dukan rashin sanin Allah da kuma muguntar mutanen da suke danne gaskiya ta wurin muguntarsu,
elements.passageText4: <sup>18</sup>18 Monnere Allah e wanga daga dow dow yimɓe ɓe kulataa Allah e dow kuuɗe maɓɓe kalluɗe ɗe ɓe nyo''irta goonga.
elements.passageText5: <sup>18</sup>For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of people who suppress the truth by their unrighteousness,

elements.question3: What does Paul say is revealed from heaven? What does it mean to ’suppress the truth’?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says that the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness. This is not a future threat only but something already being revealed — God’s settled, holy opposition to sin is an active reality in the world now. To ’suppress the truth’ means to hold down or restrain what one knows to be true — not ignorance, but deliberate resistance to truth that is available. Paul’s argument in the surrounding verses is that the existence and nature of God is visible in creation, so that people are without excuse. Suppressing the truth means choosing to push away what can be known about God in order to live without reference to Him.
elements.questionHint: The word ’suppress’ implies that the truth is already present and known — you cannot suppress something you have never encountered. What does that tell you about Paul’s assumption regarding how much people already know about God before they are told the gospel?

elements.text1: A duniya yau wanene ya ke da kasawa ta zunubi
elements.text3: In the world today, who is guilty of sinning?

elements.passageText1: <sup>9</sup>To, ƙaƙa? Mu Yahudawa mun fi sauran ne? A'a, ko kaɗan! Don dā ma mun ɗora wa Yahudawa da al'ummai laifi, cewa dukkansu zunubi yana iko da su. <sup>10</sup># Zab 14.1-3 ;  53.1-3 Kamar yadda yake a rubuce cewa, <sup>11</sup>Babu wani mai fahimta, babu wani mai neman Allah. <sup>12</sup>Duk sun bauɗe, sun zama marasa amfani baki ɗaya, <sup>23</sup>gama 'yan adam duka sun yi zunubi, sun kasa kuma ga ɗaukakar Allah.
