Mun riga mun zartar cewa da Yahudawa da Al’ummai duk suna a ƙarƙarshin zunubi. <sup>10</sup>Kamar yadda yake a rubuce cewa, <sup>11</sup>babu wani mai fahimta, <sup>12</sup>Duk sun kauce gaba ɗaya, <sup>23</sup>gama duka sun yi zunubi suka kuma kāsa ga ɗaukakar Allah,
elements.passageText4: <sup>9</sup>9 Too, ɗume mbi'eten? Enen Yahuudanko'en en ɓurii yimɓe naa' Yahuudanko'en won ɗon? Goonga naa' non. Mi yaadake mi hollii wiiki Yahuudanko'en e yimɓe naa' Yahuudanko'en ɓe fuu ley laamu hakke ɓe ngoni. <sup>10</sup>10 Non no ɗum windiraa, <sup>11</sup>11 walaa gooduɗo faamu, <sup>12</sup>12 Yimɓe fuu ngaylitanii Allah ɓaawo, ɓe ngartii walaa ko ɓe nafi. <sup>23</sup>23 Koo moye neɗɗo waɗi hakke woɗɗitake ko Allah yiɗi o waɗa.
elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>What then? Are we better off? Certainly not, for we have already charged that Jews and Greeks alike are all under sin, <sup>10</sup>just as it is written: ‘There is no one righteous, not even one, <sup>11</sup>there is no one who understands, there is no one who seeks God. <sup>12</sup>All have turned away, together they have become worthless; there is no one who shows kindness, not even one.’ . . . <sup>23</sup>for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God,

elements.question3: Paul says there is no one who is righteous. How do you respond to that claim? Does it feel too sweeping?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s claim is deliberately absolute and universal — ‘not even one’ leaves no room for exceptions. Many people’s instinctive reaction is that this feels too sweeping: surely some people are genuinely good? But Paul is not saying no one ever does anything kind or admirable. He is saying that no one, measured against God’s standard, is righteous — no one fully seeks God, no one consistently does what is right in God’s eyes. The phrase ’fall short of the glory of God’ in verse 23 captures this: it is not that some people fall slightly short and others massively so — all of us fall short. The point is not to rank human behaviour but to establish that the problem of sin is universal, which is the necessary foundation for understanding why the gospel applies to everyone.
elements.questionHint: Paul says ’not even one’ is righteous — he is not comparing people to each other but to God’s standard. How might the conclusion change if the measuring stick is God’s perfect character rather than a comparison with other human beings?

elements.text1: Menene sakamakon zunubin mu?
elements.text3: What is the consequence of our sin?

elements.passageText1: <sup>23</sup>Gama sakamakon zunubi mutuwa ne, amma baiwar Allah ita ce rai madawwami a cikin Almasihu Yesu Ubangijinmu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>23</sup>Gama sakamakon zunubi mutuwa ne, amma kyautar Allah rai madawwami ne a cikin Kiristi Yesu Ubangijinmu.
elements.passageText4: <sup>23</sup>23 Ngam njoɓaari hakke ɗum mayde, ammaa caahu Allah woni yonki ki re'ataa keɓeteeki barka sumpootirki e Yeesu Almasiihu Joomiraawo meeɗen.
elements.passageText5: <sup>23</sup>For the payoff of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.

elements.question3: What does Paul say sin earns? What contrast does he draw at the end of the verse?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says the payoff — the earned wage — of sin is death. He uses the language of wages deliberately: death is not an arbitrary punishment but the just return for a life of sin, what sin deserves and earns. The contrast he draws at the end of the verse is striking and carefully constructed: against the wages of sin (something earned and deserved) he places the gift of God (something freely given and unearned). Death is what we earn; eternal life is what God gives. The two sides of the verse could not be more different — one is justice, the other is grace. And both are concentrated in the person of Jesus Christ, in whose name the gift is given.
elements.questionHint: Paul deliberately uses the word ’payoff’ or ’wages’ for what sin produces — something that is earned — and then contrasts it with a ’gift’ for what God offers. Why is that distinction between earning and receiving as a gift so important to the point he is making?

elements.text1: Wacce sa zuciya ko bege ya rage mana?
elements.text3: What is the only hope for us?

elements.passageText1: <sup>21</sup>Sai na ga, ashe, ya zama mini ka'ida, in na so yin abin da yake daidai, sai in ga mugunta tare da ni. <sup>22</sup>Ni kam, a birnin zuciyata, na amince da shari'ar Allah. <sup>23</sup>Amma ina ganin wata ka'ida dabam a gaɓoɓina, wadda take yaƙi da ka'idar da hankalina ya ɗauka, har tana mai da ni bawan ƙa'idar nan ta zunubi wadda take zaune a gaɓoɓina. <sup>24</sup>Kaitona! Wa zai cece ni daga jikin nan, wanda yake kai ni ga mutuwa? <sup>25</sup>Godiya tā tabbata ga Allah, akwai, ta wurin Yesu Almasihu Ubangijinmu! Ashe kuwa, ni a kaina, da hankalina, hakika Shari'ar Allah nake bauta wa, amma da jikina ka'idar zunubi nake bauta wa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>21</sup>Saboda haka na gane wannan ƙa’ida ce take aiki a cikina. Sa’ad da nake so in yi abu mai kyau, sai in ga cewa mugun nan ne nake yi. <sup>22</sup>Gama a ciki-cikina ina farin cikin da dokar Allah; <sup>23</sup>amma ina ganin wata doka tana aiki a gaɓoɓin jikina, tana yaƙi da dokar hankalina, tana kuma sa in zama ɗaurarren dokar zunubin da take aiki a gaɓoɓin jikina. <sup>24</sup>Kaitona! Wane ne zai cece ni daga wannan jiki mai mutuwa? <sup>25</sup>Godiya ga Allah ta wurin Yesu Kiristi Ubangijinmu!
elements.passageText4: <sup>21</sup>21 Ɗum wartaniiyam ngikku, fuu carel ko ngiɗumi waɗuki goɗɗum boɗɗum, sey goɗɗum kalluɗum gonɗum nder am haɗayam waɗukiɗum. <sup>22</sup>22 Daga nder ɓernde am e mi nana belɗum dooka ƴiwoyɗum daga to Allah. <sup>23</sup>23 Ammaa nder ɓandu am mi yi'ii goɗɗum dooka feere e honida e dooka ɗum ɓernde am yiɗi. Ɗum wartiriiyam maccuɗo hakke gonɗum nder ɓandu am. <sup>24</sup>24 Mi neɗɗo jurminiiɗo! Moye hisnatayam daga ndu'u ɓandu am pooɗanaynduyam halkere? <sup>25</sup>25 Yettoore tabbita to Allah barka Joomiraawo meeɗen Yeesu Almasiihu!
elements.passageText5: <sup>21</sup>So I find this law at work: When I want to do good, evil is present with me. <sup>22</sup>For I delight in the law of God in my inner being. <sup>23</sup>But I see a different law in my members waging war against the law of my mind and making me captive to the law of sin that is in my members. <sup>24</sup>Wretched man that I am! Who will rescue me from this body of death? <sup>25</sup>Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, I myself serve the law of God with my mind, but with my flesh I serve the law of sin.

elements.question3: Paul describes an internal battle. Have you ever experienced what he describes — wanting to do good but finding yourself doing otherwise? Where does he say the rescue comes from?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul describes an experience many people recognise: the frustrating gap between knowing what is right and actually doing it, between intentions and actions. He does not describe a person indifferent to goodness — he describes someone who genuinely wants to do good and delights in God’s law, yet finds an opposing force within them winning the battle. His cry of ’wretched man that I am!’ is one of the most honest outbursts in the Bible. Crucially, he does not look to willpower, self-discipline, or moral effort for rescue — his question is ’who will rescue me?’, not ’what must I do?’ The answer is immediate: rescue comes through Jesus Christ. The solution to the internal battle is not trying harder but being rescued by another.
elements.questionHint: Paul frames his question as ’who will rescue me?’ rather than ’what should I do to improve myself?’ — what does the choice of that word ’rescue’ tell you about whether Paul thinks the answer to sin lies within himself or outside himself?

elements.text1: Mabuɗin gaskiya: Zunubi tayarwa ce ga Allah.
elements.text3: The picture here is bleak — but it is honest. We cannot fix this ourselves. The good news is that God does not leave us without hope, which is exactly where the next study begins.

elements.text1: Gaskiya al'amari
elements.text3: Key truths

elements.text1:
Zunubi tayarwa ce ga Allah. Ya kuma shigo ne sanadiyar mutanen farko, Adamu da Hauwa'u, a yayi da dagangan cikin sakaci suka ƙi bin umarnin Allah.

'Matar ta ga yadda itacen yana da kyau, 'ya'yansa kuma kyawawa, abin sha'awa ne kuma ga ido, abin marmari ne domin ba da hikima, ta tsinka daga 'ya'yansa, ta kuwa ci, ta kuma bai wa mijinta waɗansu, shi kuma ya ci.'

Farawa 3:6
elements.text3:
Sin is rebellion against God. It came into the world through the first man and woman, Adam and Eve, as they deliberately disobeyed God.

"When the woman saw that the fruit of the tree was good for food and pleasing to the eye, and also desirable for gaining wisdom, she took some and ate it. She also gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate it." — Genesis 3:6

elements.text1:
Duk wani ɗan adam in dai har ya taɓa rayuwa to ya yi wa Allah zunubi.

'…gama 'yan adam duka sun yi zunubi, sun kasa kuma ga ɗaukakar Allah….'

Romawa 3:23
elements.text3:
Every human being who has ever lived has sinned against God.

"...for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God..." — Romans 3:23

elements.text1: Abubuwa tunani akai
elements.text3: To think about

elements.question1: Tayaya zaka/ki iya yiwa aboki/ƙawa bayanin ma'anar zunubi bisa ga koyarwar Littafi Mai Tsarki?
elements.question3: How would you explain to a friend the meaning of sin according to what the Bible teaches?

elements.question1: Menene abokan ka/ki suke tunani game da zunubi? Shin suna tunanin su kansu sun yi zunubi?
elements.question3: What do your friends think about sin? Do they think that they sin themselves?

elements.text1: A binciken mu na ƙarshe mun koyi abinda Allah ya ke tunani game da zunubi da kuma sakamakon zunubin mu. Cikin wannan binciken zamu fara duba shirin Allah domin ceton mu, maimakon hukunta mu.
elements.text3: In the last study we saw the seriousness of sin and its consequences. The picture was bleak. But God does not leave us there. This study is where the good news begins — we look at God’s extraordinary plan not to punish us, but to rescue us.

elements.text1: Menene Allah yake tunani game da duniyar nan tasa?
elements.text3: What does God think about His world?

elements.passageText1: <sup>16</sup>“Saboda ƙaunar da Allah ya yi wa duniya har ya ba da makaɗaicin Ɗansa, domin duk wanda ya gaskata da shi kada ya hallaka, sai dai ya sami rai madawwami.
elements.passageText3: <sup>16</sup>Gama Allah ya ƙaunaci duniya sosai har ya ba da wanda yake Ɗaya kuma Makaɗaicin Ɗansa domin duk wanda ya gaskata da shi kada yă hallaka sai dai yă sami rai madawwami.
elements.passageText4: <sup>16</sup>16 Ngam yidde nde Allah yiɗi duuniyaaru haa o hokkii Ɓiɗɗo maako bajjo # 3.16:  Bajjo woni ɓiɗɗo danyaaɗo kanko tan. O walaa minyiraawo koo mawniraawo. , ngam koo moye kooliiɗomo to' halka ammaa heɓa yonki ki re'ataa.
elements.passageText5: <sup>16</sup>For this is the way God loved the world: He gave his one and only Son, so that everyone who believes in him will not perish but have eternal life.

elements.question3: What does this verse tell us about God’s attitude towards the world, despite the sin we looked at in the last study?
elements.sampleAnswer: Despite the world’s sin, God’s attitude is one of deep love — not anger or rejection. His love was so great that it moved him to action: he gave the most precious thing he had, his one and only Son. And the purpose of that gift was not punishment but rescue — so that anyone who believes would not perish but have eternal life. God’s response to human sinfulness is therefore not condemnation but an offer of salvation.
elements.questionHint: What word does John use to describe God’s motivation, and what did that motivation lead God to do?

elements.text1: Menene Allah ya sa ya aiki Ɗan sa, Yesu, cikin duniya?
elements.text3: Why did God send His Son, Jesus, into the world?

elements.passageText1: <sup>45</sup>Domin Ɗan Mutum ma ya zo ba domin a bauta masa ba, sai dai domin shi ya yi bautar, ya kuma ba da ransa fansa saboda mutane da yawa.”
elements.passageText3: <sup>45</sup>Gama ko Ɗan Mutum ma, bai zo domin a bauta masa ba, amma don yă yi bauta, yă kuma ba da ransa fansa saboda mutane da yawa.”
elements.passageText4: <sup>45</sup>45 Ngam Ɓii Neɗɗo warii naa' ngam o jagganee, ammaa ngam o jagganoo yimɓe, o hokka yonki maako ngam rimɗinki ɗuɗɓe.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>45</sup>For even the Son of Man did not come to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.

elements.question3: How does Jesus describe his own purpose? What does the word ’ransom’ imply about our situation?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus describes his purpose as coming not to be served but to serve, and specifically to give his life as a ransom for many. The word ’ransom’ implies that we are in a state of captivity or debt that we cannot pay ourselves — someone is held, and a price must be paid to secure their release. Applied to us, it means we are bound by sin and its penalty, and Jesus willingly gave his own life as the payment that sets us free.
elements.questionHint: Think about what a ransom is used for in everyday life — what does that tell you about the condition of the people Jesus came to help?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Amma kuwa Allah yana tabbatar mana da ƙaunar da yake yi mana, wato tun muna masu zunubi, Almasihu ya mutu dominmu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>8</sup>Amma Allah ya nuna mana ƙaunarsa a wannan. Tun muna masu zunubi tukuna, Kiristi ya mutu dominmu.
elements.passageText4: <sup>8</sup>8 Ammaa laawol ngol Allah hollii'en yidde maako woni, carel ko ngonɗen nder hakke, Almasiihu maayii ngam meeɗen.
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>But God demonstrates his own love for us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

elements.question3: What makes God’s love here particularly striking, according to Paul?
elements.sampleAnswer: What makes God’s love striking is the timing: Christ died for us while we were still sinners. We had not cleaned up our act, turned to God, or earned his favour in any way. Normally we might expect someone to sacrifice themselves for a good or deserving person — but God demonstrated his love at the very point when we were least deserving of it. The love is unconditional and entirely one-directional; it comes from God’s character, not from anything in us.
elements.questionHint: Notice the word ’while’ — at what point in our moral condition did Christ die, and why does the timing matter?

elements.passageText1: <sup>15</sup>Maganar nan tabbatacciya ce, ta kuma cancanci a karɓe ta ɗungum cewa, “Almasihu Yesu ya shigo duniya ne domin ceton masu zunubi,” ni ne kuwa babbansu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>15</sup>Ga wata magana tabbatacciya wadda ta cancanci cikakkiyar karɓa. Kiristi Yesu ya zo duniya domin ceton masu zunubi, wanda nake mafi muni a cikinsu.
elements.passageText4: <sup>15</sup>15 Yeesu Almasiihu warii nder duuniyaaru ngam hisna gaɗooɓe hakke. Haala ka'a ka tabbutuka ka hewtii ɗum jaɓaka. Min boo, mi ɓurii koo moye waɗuki hakke.
elements.passageText5: <sup>15</sup>This saying is trustworthy and deserves full acceptance: ‘Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners’—and I am the worst of them!

elements.question3: Paul calls himself ’the worst’ of sinners. Why do you think he says this? What does it tell us about who Jesus came to save?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul is probably referring to his past as a violent persecutor of Christians — someone who had actively hunted down and imprisoned followers of Jesus. He does not say this to be falsely modest, but to make a point: if Christ came to save even him, then no one is beyond reach. It tells us that Jesus did not come only for the morally respectable or the mildly flawed — he came to save sinners, full stop. The scope of salvation is as wide as the depth of human sinfulness.
elements.questionHint: If even the ’worst’ sinner is included in Christ’s saving purpose, what does that imply about whether anyone could be considered too sinful to be saved?

elements.text1: Sakamakon mutuwar Yesu
elements.text3: What is the result of Jesus’ death?

elements.passageText1: <sup>9</sup>To, tun da yake a yanzu an kuɓutar da mu ta wurin jininsa, ashe kuwa, ta wurinsa za mu fi samun kārewa daga fushin Allah ke nan.
elements.passageText3: <sup>9</sup>Da yake yanzu mun sami zama marasa laifi ta wurin jininsa, ashe kuwa za a cece mu daga fushin Allah ta wurinsa!
elements.passageText4: <sup>9</sup>9 Nde jooni en ngartii aadili'en barka ƴiiƴam # 5.9:  Waato mayde maako.  Almasiihu, fii ɓurɗum tabbutuki woni o hisnay'en daga monnere Allah garaynde.
elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>Much more then, because we have now been declared righteous by his blood, we will be saved through him from God’s wrath.

elements.question3: According to Romans 5:9, what are those who trust in Christ declared to be, and what are they saved from?
elements.sampleAnswer: According to Romans 5:9, those who trust in Christ are declared righteous — a legal verdict made possible by Jesus’ blood (his atoning death). And as a result of that declaration, they are saved from God’s wrath. This is significant: God’s wrath is his righteous judgment against sin, which we all deserve. But through Christ, that judgment has been dealt with, and believers are no longer under its threat.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses a courtroom-style word — what verdict is pronounced over believers, and what danger does that verdict protect them from?

elements.passageText1: <sup>16</sup>“Saboda ƙaunar da Allah ya yi wa duniya har ya ba da makaɗaicin Ɗansa, domin duk wanda ya gaskata da shi kada ya hallaka, sai dai ya sami rai madawwami. <sup>17</sup>Gama Allah bai aiko Ɗansa duniya domin yă yanke mata hukunci ba, sai dai domin duniya ta sami ceto ta wurinsa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>16</sup>Gama Allah ya ƙaunaci duniya sosai har ya ba da wanda yake Ɗaya kuma Makaɗaicin Ɗansa domin duk wanda ya gaskata da shi kada yă hallaka sai dai yă sami rai madawwami. <sup>17</sup>Gama Allah bai aiki Ɗansa duniya don yă yi wa duniya hukunci ba, sai dai domin yă ceci duniya ta wurinsa.
elements.passageText4: <sup>16</sup>16 Ngam yidde nde Allah yiɗi duuniyaaru haa o hokkii Ɓiɗɗo maako bajjo # 3.16:  Bajjo woni ɓiɗɗo danyaaɗo kanko tan. O walaa minyiraawo koo mawniraawo. , ngam koo moye kooliiɗomo to' halka ammaa heɓa yonki ki re'ataa. <sup>17</sup>17 Ngam Allah liloyaayi Ɓiɗɗo muuɗum nder duuniyaaru ngam o taƴanandu kiita. Ammaa o liloyiimo ngam o hisna duuniyaaru e laawol maako.
elements.passageText5: <sup>16</sup>For this is the way God loved the world: He gave his one and only Son, so that everyone who believes in him will not perish but have eternal life. <sup>17</sup>For God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world should be saved through him.

elements.question3: What does Jesus promise to all who believe in him, according to John 3:16-17?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus promises that everyone who believes in him will not perish but will have eternal life. Verse 17 adds to this by clarifying God’s intention: he did not send his Son to condemn but to save. So believers gain both a positive gift — eternal life — and an escape from a negative outcome — condemnation and perishing. The promise is open to everyone (‘everyone who believes’), and it comes directly through faith in God’s Son.
elements.questionHint: Jesus gives two outcomes for those who believe — one thing they escape and one thing they receive. Can you identify both?

elements.text1: Menene Littafi Mai Tsarki yace game da waɗan da suka ƙi bada gaskiya ga Yesu?
elements.text3: What does the Bible say about those who do not believe in Jesus?

elements.passageText1: <sup>18</sup>Duk wanda ya gaskata da shi, ba za a yi masa hukunci ba. Wanda kuwa bai ba da gaskiya ba, an riga an yi masa hukunci, domin bai gaskata da sunan makaɗaicin Ɗan Allah ba.
elements.passageText3: <sup>18</sup>Duk wanda ya gaskata da shi ba za a yi masa hukunci ba, amma duk wanda bai gaskata ba an riga an yi masa hukunci domin bai gaskata da sunan wanda yake Ɗaya kuma Makaɗaicin Ɗan Allah ba.
elements.passageText4: <sup>18</sup>18 “Koo moye kooliiɗomo taƴantaake kiita, ammaa mo hoolaakimo o yaadake o taƴanaama kiita, ngam o hoolaaki Ɓiɗɗo Allah bajjo.
elements.passageText5: <sup>18</sup>The one who believes in him is not condemned. The one who does not believe has been condemned already, because he has not believed in the name of the one and only Son of God.

elements.question3: Jesus draws a sharp distinction here. What is the difference between the situation of the one who believes and the one who does not?
elements.sampleAnswer: The believer is not condemned — the verdict against them has been lifted through faith in Jesus. The non-believer, however, stands condemned already — not as a future possibility but as a present reality. Crucially, the condemnation is not because of a list of specific sins but because of a single issue: not believing in the name of God’s one and only Son. Jesus presents these as two completely different standing before God, with belief in him as the decisive factor that separates them.
elements.questionHint: Notice that John says the unbeliever ’has been condemned already’ — what does the tense of that phrase suggest about when this judgment takes effect?

elements.passageText1: <sup>5</sup>Wannan kuwa ita ce tabbatar hukunci mai adalci na Allah, domin a lasafta ku a cikin waɗanda suka cancanci zama a Mulkin Allah, wanda kuke shan wuya dominsa, <sup>6</sup>domin kuwa Allah ya ga adalci ne, yă yi sakamokon ƙunci ga waɗanda suke ƙuntata muku, <sup>7</sup>yă kuma ba ku hutu har da mu ma, ku da ake ƙuntata wa ɗin, a ranar bayyanar Ubangiji Yesu daga Sama tare da manyan mala'ikunsa, a cikin wuta mai ruruwa, <sup>8</sup>yana ta sāka wa waɗanda suke ƙi sanin Allah, da kuma waɗanda suka ƙi bin bisharar Ubangijinmu Yesu. <sup>9</sup># Ish 2.10  Za su sha hukuncin madawwamiyar hallaka, suna a ware da zatin Ubangiji, da kuma, maɗaukakin ikonsa, <sup>10</sup>wato, a lokacin da ya dawo, tsarkakansa su ɗaukaka shi, duk masu ba da gaskiya kuma su yi al'ajabinsa a ranar nan, don kun gaskata shaidar da muka yi muku.
elements.passageText3: <sup>5</sup>Dukan wannan yana tabbatar cewa hukuncin Allah daidai ne, saboda haka kuma za a lissafta ku a kan kun cancanci mulkin Allah, wanda dominsa ne kuke shan wahala. <sup>6</sup>Allah mai adalci ne, Zai sāka wa masu ba ku wahala da wahala <sup>7</sup>yă kuma sauƙaƙa muku ku da kuke wahala, har ma da mu. Wannan zai faru sa’ad da aka bayyana Ubangiji Yesu daga sama cikin wuta mai ci tare da mala’ikunsa masu iko. <sup>8</sup>Zai hukunta waɗanda ba su san Allah ba da kuma waɗanda ba su yi biyayya da bisharar Ubangijinmu Yesu ba. <sup>9</sup>Za a hukunta su da madawwamiyar hallaka a kuma kau da su daga gaban Ubangiji da kuma daga ɗaukakar ikonsa <sup>10</sup>a ranar da zai dawo don a ɗaukaka shi a cikin mutanensa masu tsarki, yă kuma zama abin banmamaki ga dukan waɗanda suka gaskata. Wannan ya haɗa har da ku, domin kun gaskata shaidar da muka yi muku.
elements.passageText4: <sup>5</sup>5 Fuu ɗu'um tabbitinii kiita Allah e woodi goonga, ngam maajum ɗum wartiray'on kewtuɓe jooɗaaki nder laamu maako, ngam dow maajum njaroton torra. <sup>6</sup>6 Allah o gaɗoowo ko haani. O waatanay ɓen ɗon torrayɓe'on e torra, <sup>7</sup>7 o waltinay ɓerɗe mooɗon onon torreteeɓe. Minon maa o waltinay ɓerɗe amin. O waɗay non nyalnde nde Yeesu Almasiihu wangata daga dow nder hiite hawti e malaa'ika'en maako gooduɓe bawɗe. <sup>8</sup>8 Ganyuɓe Allah e ganyuɓe ngaɗa ko Habaru Belɗum ɗum Joomiraawo meeɗen Yeesu wi'i, Allah waatanayɓe halkere kallunde. <sup>9</sup>9 Ɓe njaray torra halkere nde haa abadaa, ɗum ittayɓe daga yeeso Joomiraawo e yeeso mangu bawɗe maako. # Ica 2.10. <sup>10</sup>10 Halkere nden wangay nyalnde nde o wartoyta. O wartoyay ngam ceniiɓe maako teddinamo fuu kooliiɓemo mawninamo. Onon maa on kawtay e maɓɓe, ngam on njaɓii habaru ɗum min kokki'on.
elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>This is evidence of God’s righteous judgment, to make you worthy of the kingdom of God, for which you are suffering. <sup>6</sup>For it is right for God to repay with affliction those who afflict you, <sup>7</sup>and to give relief to you who are afflicted and to us as well, when the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his mighty angels. <sup>8</sup>With flaming fire he will mete out punishment on those who do not know God and do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus. <sup>9</sup>They will undergo the penalty of eternal destruction, away from the presence of the Lord and from the glory of his strength, <sup>10</sup>when he comes to be glorified among his saints and admired on that day among all who have believed—and you did believe our testimony to you.

elements.question3: How does Paul describe the fate of those who do not know God and do not obey the gospel? How does this sit with you?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul describes their fate as ’eternal destruction, away from the presence of the Lord and from the glory of his strength.’ This is not annihilation but permanent separation from God — the source of all goodness, light, and life. It is a sobering description, and many people find it deeply uncomfortable. It raises hard questions about fairness and the character of God. Yet Paul frames it within God’s righteous justice: it is a right response to those who have rejected both God and the gospel. How it sits with us personally will vary, and that honest reaction is worth exploring.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses a specific phrase to describe what this destruction means relationally — what are those people separated from, and why might that detail matter more than the word ’destruction’ alone?

elements.text1: Tayaya mutuwar Yesu ta cece mu?
elements.text3: How does Jesus’ death save us?

elements.passageText1: <sup>46</sup># Zab 22.1  Wajen ƙarfe uku kuma sai Yesu ya ɗaga murya da ƙarfi ya ce, “Eloi, Eloi, lama sabaktāni?” Wato, “Ya Allahna, ya Allahna, don me ka yashe ni?”
elements.passageText3: <sup>46</sup>Wajen sa’a ta tara, Yesu ya ɗaga murya da ƙarfi ya ce,  “Eloi, Eloi, # 27.46  Waɗansu rubuce-rubucen hannu na dā suna da  Eloi, Eloi   lama sabaktani?”  (Wato,  “Ya Allahna, ya Allahna, don me ka yashe ni?” ). # 27.46  Zab 22.1
elements.passageText4: <sup>46</sup>46 E njamndi tati ndin, sey Yeesu wi'i e sembe, “Eloy, Eloy, lama sabattani?” waato, “Yaa Allah am, Yaa Allah am, ngam ɗume cendirɗaa e am?” # Jab 22.1.
elements.passageText5: 46At about three o’clock Jesus shouted with a loud voice, ‘Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani?’ (that is, ‘My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?’).

elements.question3: What does Jesus cry out from the cross? What might it mean that the Son of God was ’forsaken’ by his Father at this moment?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus cries out ’My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?’ — a cry of real abandonment, not just distress. For the eternal Son who had always existed in perfect unity with the Father, this forsakenness represents something almost unthinkable: a rupture in that relationship. Theologically, it points to Jesus bearing the full weight of sin’s consequence — separation from God — in our place. The judgment that should fall on us for our sin fell on him instead, which is why he experienced what we deserved.
elements.questionHint: Given what we have already seen about the consequence of sin (separation from God), what might it mean that Jesus experienced that same separation on the cross?

elements.passageText1: <sup>21</sup>Shi da bai san zunubi ba, Allah ya mai da shi zunubi saboda mu, domin mu mu sami adalcin Allah ta wurinsa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>21</sup>Shi da bai san zunubi ba, Allah ya mai da shi kamar hadayar zunubi saboda mu, domin mu sami adalcin Allah ta wurinsa.
elements.passageText4: <sup>21</sup>21 Almasiihu meeɗaayi waɗuki hakke, ammaa Allah yowii fuu hakkeeji meeɗen dow maako, ngam laawol maako ngarten aadili'en yeeso Allah.
elements.passageText5: <sup>21</sup>God made the one who did not know sin to be sin for us, so that in him we would become the righteousness of God.

elements.question3: What exchange does Paul describe in this verse? What did Jesus take on, and what do believers receive?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul describes a remarkable double exchange. Jesus, who had no sin of his own (‘the one who did not know sin’), was made to be sin for us — he took on our sin and its full consequence. In return, believers receive the righteousness of God — not a righteousness they earned, but one that belongs to God himself, credited to them through union with Christ. It is a swap of opposites: our sin goes to him; his righteousness comes to us.
elements.questionHint: Paul says Jesus ’did not know sin’ — so what makes the exchange he describes so surprising, and what does each party in the exchange give and receive?

elements.passageText1: <sup>13</sup># M.Sh 21.23  Almasihu ya fanso mu daga la'anar nan ta Shari'a, da ya zama abin la'ana saboda mu, domin a rubuce yake cewa, “Duk wanda aka kafa a jikin itace, la'ananne ne.”
elements.passageText3: <sup>13</sup>Kiristi ya fanshe mu daga la’anar Doka ta wurin zama la’ana saboda mu, gama a rubuce yake cewa, “La’ananne ne duk wanda aka rataye a kan itace.” # 3.13  M Sh 21.23
elements.passageText4: <sup>13</sup>13 Yeesu Almasiihu rimɗinii'en daga laaneeki ki Attawra waddi, nde o warti ko laanaa ngam meeɗen. Ngam aayaare wi'ii, “Koo moye baraaɗo dow lekki # 3.13:  Waato gaafaangal.  o laanaaɗo.” # TA 21.23.
elements.passageText5: <sup>13</sup>Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us (for it is written, ‘Cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree’);

elements.question3: What ’curse’ does Paul say Christ took on? What does it mean that he ’redeemed’ us from it?
elements.sampleAnswer: The curse Paul refers to is the curse of the law — the judgment and condemnation that falls on everyone who fails to keep God’s law perfectly (which is all of us). Christ took that curse on himself by hanging on a cross, which the Old Testament specifically associated with being cursed by God. To ’redeem’ us means to buy us out of that condition — like purchasing the freedom of a slave. Christ paid the price of the curse so that we would no longer be under it.
elements.questionHint: What does it mean to ’redeem’ something in everyday life (think of redeeming a voucher or buying something back), and how does that shed light on what Christ did for us?

elements.passageText1: <sup>24</sup># Ish 53.5 , 6  Shi kansa ya ɗauke zunubanmu a jikinsa a kan gungume, domin mu yi zamanmu matattu ga zunubi, rayayyu kuma ga adalci. Da raunukansa ne aka warkar da ku.
elements.passageText3: <sup>24</sup>Shi kansa ya ɗauki zunubanmu a jikinsa a kan itace, domin mu mutu ga zunubai mu kuma rayu ga adalci; ta wurin raunukansa ne kuka sami warkarwa.
elements.passageText4: <sup>24</sup>24 Kanko e hoore maako jaɓi o yowanee hakke ɗum ngaɗuɗen o ɓilee dow gaafaangal. O waɗu non ngam laawol mayde maako alen waɗuki hakke, njooɗooɗen jonde aadilaaku. No aayaare wi'i, “Barka nawnooɗe maako on njamɗitii.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>24</sup>He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree, that we may cease from sinning and live for righteousness. By his wounds you were healed.

elements.question3: What did Jesus bear on the cross according to Peter, and what does Peter say the purpose of this was for us?
elements.sampleAnswer: Peter says Jesus bore our sins in his body on the cross — he physically and personally carried them. The purpose was twofold: that we might cease from sinning (freedom from sin’s power) and live for righteousness (a new positive direction of life). Peter also adds that by Jesus’ wounds we were healed — using the language of healing to describe what the cross accomplishes spiritually. The cross is not only about forgiveness of past sin but about transformation for the future.
elements.questionHint: Peter mentions two results of Jesus bearing our sins — one is about stopping something and the other is about starting something. Can you identify what each is?

elements.text1: Ayoyin da ke sama sun bayyana abinda Yesu ke yi a kan gicciye. Ba mutuwa ta al'ada ba ce.
elements.text3: The verses above describe what Jesus was doing on the cross. He was not simply dying as a martyr or a good example — He was taking the penalty for our sin in our place. This is what makes His death different from any other death in history.

elements.text1: Don mai mutuwa bata yi nasara da Yesu ba?
elements.text3: Why was Jesus not defeated by death?

elements.passageText1: <sup>24</sup># Mat 28.5 , 6 ;  Mar 16.6 ;  Luk 24.5  Amma Allah ya tashe shi bayan ya ɓalle masa ƙangin mutuwa, domin ba shi yiwuwa mutuwa ta riƙe shi.
elements.passageText3: <sup>24</sup>Amma Allah ya tashe shi daga matattu, ya ’yantar da shi daga azabar mutuwa, don ba ya yiwuwa mutuwa ta riƙe shi.
elements.passageText4: <sup>24</sup>24 Ammaa Allah umminiimo daga mayde, wurtiniimo yenaande, ngam mayde waawataa jogaakimo. # Maat 28.5,6; Mar 16.6; Luk 24.5.
elements.passageText5: <sup>24</sup>But God raised him up, having released him from the pains of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.

elements.question3: What did God do, and why does Peter say death could not hold Jesus?
elements.sampleAnswer: God raised Jesus up, releasing him from death. Peter’s reason is striking: it was simply not possible for death to hold him. Death has no ultimate claim over the one who is the source of life itself and who had no sin of his own. The resurrection is therefore not a surprise reversal but an inevitability — death had no rightful power over Jesus once the work of bearing sin was complete.
elements.questionHint: Peter says it was ’not possible’ for death to hold Jesus — what does that strong word suggest about the relationship between Jesus and death?

elements.text1: Ya Bulus ya yi bayani akan Yesu?
elements.text3: How is Jesus described by Paul?

elements.passageText1: <sup>5</sup>Domin Allah ɗaya ne, matsakanci kuma ɗaya ne, a tsakanin Allah da mutane, Almasihu Yesu, mutum, <sup>6</sup>wanda ya ba da kansa fansa saboda kowa da kowa. An kuwa yi shaidar wannan a daidai lokacinsa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>5</sup>Gama Allah ɗaya ne, matsakanci kuma ɗaya tsakanin Allah da mutane, shi ne kuwa Kiristi Yesu, <sup>6</sup>wanda ya ba da kansa fansa domin dukan mutane, shaidar da aka bayar a daidai lokaci.
elements.passageText4: <sup>5</sup>5 Ngam Allah o go'oto, nden ciryootirɗo hakkunde Allah e yimɓe o go'oto. Kanko woni Yeesu Almasiihu, <sup>6</sup>6 kokkuɗo hoore maako ngam o rimɗina yimɓe. Kanjum woni ceyda ɗum um hokki nde carel waɗi.
elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>For there is one God and one intermediary between God and humanity, Christ Jesus, himself human, <sup>6</sup>who gave himself as a ransom for all, revealing God’s purpose at his appointed time.

elements.question3: What two things does Paul say are uniquely ’one’ according to this passage? What role does Jesus fulfil between God and humanity?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul says there is one God and one intermediary between God and humanity. The two ’ones’ are therefore God himself and the mediator — Christ Jesus. Jesus fulfils the role of intermediary or mediator: the one who stands between two parties and brings them together. He is uniquely qualified for this because he is himself human, yet also gave himself as a ransom for all. No one else bridges that gap — not religious leaders, not our own good works, only Christ.
elements.questionHint: An intermediary or mediator stands between two parties — who are the two parties here, and what does Jesus do to bring them together?

elements.question: What do you think of when you hear the word salvation?
elements.answer: ’Salvation’ can sound like old-fashioned religious language, but it simply means rescue. The Bible uses it to describe what God has done for us through Jesus — rescuing us from the consequences of sin (God’s judgement) and restoring us to a right relationship with Him. Salvation is not something we achieve through being good enough; it is something God has done and freely offers to us. Receiving it requires a response, which is what Study 8 explores.

elements.question3: How would you answer a friend who argues that a God of love would not inflict everlasting punishment on anyone (except, perhaps, the very worst criminals)?

chapters.chapterTitle: God’s Remedy: 2 The Resurrection and Ascension

elements.text1: Allah Uba ya tashi Yesu daga matattu. Ma su bi ba mataccen malami suke bautawa ba amma Mai Ceto da Ubangijin da aka tashe shi daga matattu.
elements.text3: The cross is not the end of the story. God the Father raised Jesus from the dead — and that changes everything. Christians do not worship a dead teacher; they worship a risen Saviour and living Lord. In this study we explore what the resurrection and ascension mean for us.

elements.text1: Menene ya sa tashin Yesu daga matattu ya ke da matuƙar mahimmanci
elements.text3: Why is the raising of Jesus from the dead (His resurrection) so important?

elements.passageText1: <sup>14</sup>In kuwa ba a ta da Almasihu ba, to, ashe, wa'azinmu a banza ne, bangaskiyarku kuma banza ce. <sup>17</sup>Luk 24.34 ;  Mat 28.16 , 17 ;  Mar 16.14 ;  Luk 24.36 ;  Yah 20.19 <sup>18</sup>Ashe, waɗanda suka yi barci a kan su na Almasihu, sun hallaka ke nan. <sup>19</sup>Da begenmu ga Almasihu domin wannan rai ne kaɗai, ai, da mun fi kowa zama abin tausayi.
elements.passageText3: <sup>14</sup>In ba a tashe Kiristi ba kuwa, ai, wa’azinmu, da bangaskiyarku banza ne. <sup>17</sup>In kuwa ba a tashe Kiristi ba, bangaskiyarku a banza take, ya zama har yanzu kuna cikin zunubanku ke nan. <sup>18</sup>Ashe, waɗanda suka yi barci a cikin Kiristi ma sun hallaka ke nan. <sup>19</sup>In a wannan rayuwar ce kaɗai muke da bege cikin Kiristi, mun zama abin tausayi fiye da dukan mutane.
elements.passageText4: <sup>14</sup>14 To boo Almasiihu ummaaki daga mayde, too, wa'ajinki amin wartii meere, nden hoolaare mooɗon wartii meere. <sup>17</sup>17 Daa Almasiihu umminaaka, too, hoolaare mooɗon wartii meere, nden haa jooni on ngondi e hakke mooɗon, <sup>18</sup>18 nden mayɓe e koolii Almasiihu kalkii won ɗon. <sup>19</sup>19 To ni ngam jonde duuniyaaru ndu'u tan ngaɗeten kammunde dow Almasiihu, en ɓurii koo moye wartuki ɓe ɗum yurminanta.
elements.passageText5: <sup>14</sup>And if Christ has not been raised, then our preaching is futile and your faith is empty. . . <sup>17</sup>And if Christ has not been raised, your faith is useless; you are still in your sins. <sup>18</sup>Furthermore, those who have fallen asleep in Christ have also perished. <sup>19</sup>For if only in this life we have hope in Christ, we should be pitied more than anyone.

elements.question3: Paul lists several things that would be true if Christ had not been raised. What are they? What does this tell you about how central the resurrection is to the Christian faith?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul lists four devastating consequences that would follow if Christ had not been raised: Christian preaching would be futile and empty; faith would be useless; believers would still be in their sins — unforgiven; and those who have already died as Christians would have simply perished. He adds a fifth: if our hope in Christ extends only to this life, Christians are to be pitied more than anyone, having given up much for nothing. Taken together, these show that the resurrection is not a peripheral detail but the very foundation of the Christian faith. Without it, everything else — forgiveness, eternal life, future hope — collapses.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses the word ’if’ repeatedly — try listing each consequence he names one by one, and then ask yourself what they have in common.

elements.text1: Wacce shaida akwai akan cewar Yesu ya tashi daga matattu?
elements.text3: What proof is there that Jesus rose from the dead?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>A ranar farko ta mako kuwa, da asussuba, suka je wurin kabarin da kayan ƙanshi da suka shirya. <sup>2</sup>Sai suka tarar an mirgine dutsen daga kabarin. <sup>3</sup>Suka shiga ciki, amma ba su sami jikin Ubangiji ba. <sup>4</sup>Tun suna cikin damuwa da abin, sai kawai ga waɗansu mutum biyu a tsaye kusa da su, saye da tufafi masu ƙyalƙyali. <sup>5</sup>Don tsoro, matan suka sunkuyar da kansu ƙasa. Mutanen suka ce musu, “Don me kuke neman rayayye a cikin matattu? <sup>6</sup># Mat 16.21 ;  17.22 , 23 ;  20.18 , 19 ;  Mar 8.31 ;  9.31 ;  10.33 , 34 ;  Luk 9.22 ;  18.31-33  Ai, ba ya nan, ya tashi. Ku tuna yadda ya gaya muku tun yana Galili <sup>7</sup>cewa, ‘Lalle ne a ba da Ɗan Mutum ga mutane masu zunubi, a gicciye shi, a rana ta uku kuma ya tashi’.”
elements.passageText3: <sup>1</sup>Da sassafe a ranar farko ta mako, matan suka je kabarin, da kayan ƙanshin da suka shirya. <sup>2</sup>Suka iske an riga an gungurar da dutsen daga bakin kabarin. <sup>3</sup>Amma da suka shiga ciki, ba su iske jikin Ubangiji Yesu ba. <sup>4</sup>Yayinda suna cikin tunani a kan wannan, nan take, ga waɗansu mutum biyu tsaye kusa da su, sanye da kaya masu ƙyalli, kamar walƙiya. <sup>5</sup>A cikin tsoro, matan suka fāɗi da fuskokinsu a ƙasa, amma mutanen suka ce musu, “Me ya sa kuke neman mai rai a cikin matattu? <sup>6</sup>Ba ya nan, ya tashi! Ku tuna yadda ya gaya muku tun yana tare da ku a Galili cewa, <sup>7</sup>‘Dole a ba da Ɗan Mutum ga hannun masu zunubi, a kuma gicciye shi, a rana ta uku kuma a tashe shi.’   ”
elements.passageText4: <sup>1</sup>1 Nyalnde Alal subaka, rewɓe ɓen kocci nebbam uroojam ɗam ɓe mo''ini ngam ɓe moytana ɓandu maako, ɓe njahi yenaande nden. <sup>2</sup>2 Ɓe tawi hayre nden tallitaama daga dammugal yenaande nden. <sup>3</sup>3 Ammaa nde ɓe natti, ɓe tawaayi ɓandu Yeesu. <sup>4</sup>4 E ɓe nder wannaare # 24.4:  Koo boo ɓillaare.  dow fii ɗum, sey bo maƴƴere ɓe ngi'i worɓe ɗiɗo e ndarii haade maɓɓe. E ɓe ɓornii kolte daneeje tar. <sup>5</sup>5 Kulol nangiɓe, sey ɓe njani ɓe kippii. Worɓe ɓen mbi'iɓe, “Ngam ɗume ɗaɓɓitoton mo yonki nder mayɓe? <sup>6</sup>6 O walaa ɗo'o. Ɗum umminiimo daga mayde! Ciftoree ko o wi'unoo'on ko o wondunoo e mooɗon nder leydi Galiili. <sup>7</sup>7 O wi'ii, ‘Doole Ɓii Neɗɗo hokkitiree nder juuɗe kalluɓe, o waree dow gaafaangal, nden o ummoo nder nyalnde tatawre.’ ” # Maat 16.21; 17.22,23; 20.18,19; Mar 8.31; 9.31; 10.33,34; Luk 9.22; 18.31‑33.
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>Now on the first day of the week, at early dawn, the women went to the tomb, taking the aromatic spices they had prepared. <sup>2</sup>They found that the stone had been rolled away from the tomb, <sup>3</sup>but when they went in, they did not find the body of the Lord Jesus. <sup>4</sup>While they were perplexed about this, suddenly two men stood beside them in gleaming clothing. <sup>5</sup>The women were terribly frightened and bowed their faces to the ground, but the men said to them, ‘Why do you look for the living among the dead? <sup>6</sup>He is not here, but has been raised! Remember how he told you, while he was still in Galilee, <sup>7</sup>that the Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, and be crucified, and on the third day rise again.’

elements.question3: What did the women find when they arrived at the tomb? What do the two men tell them, and what had Jesus himself predicted?
elements.sampleAnswer: The women arrived expecting to anoint a body, but found the stone already rolled away and the tomb empty — Jesus’ body was gone. Two men in gleaming clothing appeared and told them plainly: ‘He is not here, but has been raised.’ They then reminded the women of something Jesus himself had predicted while still in Galilee — that the Son of Man would be handed over, crucified, and would rise again on the third day. The empty tomb was therefore not a mystery to be explained away; it was the fulfilment of Jesus’ own prior announcement.
elements.questionHint: The two men ask the women a pointed question — what does the question ’Why do you look for the living among the dead?’ imply about who they believe Jesus to be?

elements.passageText1: <sup>36</sup>Suna cikin faɗar waɗannan abubuwa, sai ga Yesu da kansa tsaye a tsakiyarsu, ya ce musu, “Salama alaikun!” <sup>37</sup>Amma suka firgita, tsoro ya kama su, suka zaci fatalwa suke gani. <sup>38</sup>Sai ya ce musu, “Don me kuka firgita, kuke kuma tantama a zuciyarku? <sup>39</sup>Ku dubi hannuwana da ƙafafuna, ai, ni ne da kaina. Ku taɓa ni, ku ji, don fatalwa ba ta da nama da ƙashi, yadda kuke gani nake da su.” <sup>40</sup>Da ya faɗi haka, ya nuna musu hannuwansa da ƙafafunsa. <sup>41</sup>Amma tun suna da sauran shakka saboda tsananin farin ciki da mamaki, sai ya ce musu, “Kuna da wani abinci a nan?” <sup>42</sup>Sai suka ba shi wata tsokar gasasshen kifi. <sup>43</sup>Ya kuwa karɓa, ya ci a gabansu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>36</sup>Suna cikin wannan zance, sai ga Yesu da kansa tsaye a tsakiyarsu. Sai ya ce musu,  “Salama a gare ku!” <sup>37</sup>Suka firgita, suka tsorota, suna tsammani fatalwa ce suka gani. <sup>38</sup>Sai ya ce musu,  “Don me kuke damuwa, kuke kuma shakka a zuciyarku? <sup>39</sup>Ku duba hannuwana da ƙafafuna. Ai, ni ne da kaina! Ku taɓa ni ku ji, ai, fatalwa ba ta da nama da ƙashi, yadda kuke gani nake da su.” <sup>40</sup>Da ya faɗi haka, sai ya nuna musu hannuwansa da ƙafafunsa. <sup>41</sup>Kuma har yanzu, suna cikin rashin gaskatawa, saboda murna da mamaki, sai ya tambaye su,  “Kuna da abinci a nan?” <sup>42</sup>Suka ba shi ɗan gasasshen kifi, <sup>43</sup>ya karɓa ya ci a gabansu.
elements.passageText4: <sup>36</sup>36 E ɓe nder metuki haala kan, sey ɗon e ɗon Yeesu e hoore muuɗum wangi darii caka maɓɓe. O wi'iɓe, “E mi silminoo. Jam wonda e mooɗon.” <sup>37</sup>37 Ɓerɗe maɓɓe taƴi, ɓe kammii ginnol ɓe ngi'i. <sup>38</sup>38 Ammaa o wi'iɓe, “Ɗume wanni'on? Ngam ɗume ngaɗoton seko nder ɓerɗe mooɗon? <sup>39</sup>39 Ndaaree juuɗe am e koyɗe am, ɗum min e hoore am. Meemeeyam nanon, ay ginnol walaa ɓandu e ƴi'e no ngiiruɗonyam.” <sup>40</sup>40 Nde o wi'i non, o holliɓe juuɗe maako e koyɗe maako. <sup>41</sup>41 Ɓe njaɓaayi ɗum goonga ngam belɗum e hayɗinki. Sey o wi'iɓe, “On ngoodi goɗɗum ko nyaametee ɗo'o?” <sup>42</sup>42 Sey ɓe kokkimo tayre lingu njuɗaangu, <sup>43</sup>43 o hoccingu o nyaami yeeso maɓɓe.
elements.passageText5: <sup>36</sup>While they were saying these things, Jesus himself stood among them and said to them, ‘Peace be with you.’ <sup>37</sup>But they were startled and terrified, thinking they saw a spirit. <sup>38</sup>Then he said to them, ‘Why are you frightened, and why do doubts arise in your hearts? <sup>39</sup>Look at my hands and my feet; it’s me myself! Touch me and see; a spirit does not have flesh and bones like you see I have.’ <sup>40</sup>And when he had said this, he showed them his hands and his feet. <sup>41</sup>Now while they still could not believe it because of their joy and were amazing themselves, he said to them, ‘Do you have anything here to eat?’ <sup>42</sup>So they gave him a piece of broiled fish, <sup>43</sup>and he took it and ate it in their presence.

elements.question3: How does Jesus demonstrate to the disciples that he is not a ghost or a vision? What details in this account suggest the resurrection was physical and bodily?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus addresses their assumption that he is a spirit directly: he invites them to look at and touch his hands and feet, and explicitly states that ’a spirit does not have flesh and bones like you see I have.’ He shows them his physical wounds. Then, to remove any remaining doubt, he asks for food and eats a piece of broiled fish in front of them. Ghosts do not eat. Each detail — the tangible body, the visible wounds, the capacity to consume food — underlines that this was a real, bodily resurrection, not a vision, hallucination, or spiritual apparition.
elements.questionHint: Jesus gives the disciples three different ways to verify that he is physically present — can you identify each one, and why might each type of evidence matter?

elements.passageText1: <sup>19</sup>A ran nan da magariba, ranar farko ta mako, ƙofofin ɗakin da almajiran suke ciki na kulle don tsoron Yahudawa, sai ga Yesu ya zo ya tsaya a tsaka, ya ce musu, “Salama alaikun!” <sup>20</sup>Da ya faɗi haka, ya nuna musu hannuwansa da kwiɓinsa. Sai almajiran suka yi farin ciki da ganin Ubangiji.
elements.passageText3: <sup>19</sup>Da Yamman ranar farko ta mako, sa’ad da almajiran suna tare, ƙofofi kuma suna a kulle saboda tsoron Yahudawa, sai ga Yesu ya zo ya tsaya a tsakiyarsu ya ce,  “Salama tă kasance tare da ku!” <sup>20</sup>Bayan ya faɗa haka, sai ya nuna musu hannuwansa da gefensa. Sai almajiran suka yi farin ciki ƙwarai da suka ga Ubangiji.
elements.passageText4: <sup>19</sup>19 Ɗum nyalnde Alal. Nde naange hiiri, tokkuɓe Yeesu kawriti, ɓe maɓɓi dammugal ngam e ɓe kula ardiiɓe Yahuudanko'en. Ɗon e ɗon Yeesu wangi darii caka maɓɓe, wi'i, “E mi silminoo. Jam wonda e mooɗon.” <sup>20</sup>20 Gaɗa o wi'i non, o holliɓe juuɗe maako e becce maako. Sey tokkuɓemo nani belɗum naa' seɗɗa nde ɓe ngi'imo.
elements.passageText5: <sup>19</sup>On the evening of that day, the first day of the week, the disciples had gathered together and locked the doors of the place because they were afraid of the Jewish leaders. Jesus came and stood among them and said to them, ‘Peace be with you.’ <sup>20</sup>When he had said this, he showed them his hands and his side. Then the disciples rejoiced when they saw the Lord.

elements.question3: The disciples were hiding behind locked doors in fear. How did their emotional state change when Jesus appeared? What significance do you see in him showing them his wounds?
elements.sampleAnswer: The transformation is striking: they went from fear — hiding behind locked doors, afraid of arrest — to rejoicing, simply because they saw the Lord. The change was immediate and total. As for the wounds: Jesus did not appear in a glorified body with no trace of the cross. He showed them his hands and his side — the marks of the nails and the spear. This confirms his identity beyond doubt (this is the same Jesus who was crucified), and it shows that the resurrection did not erase what he suffered. His wounds are permanent marks of his sacrifice.
elements.questionHint: Think about why Jesus specifically showed his wounds rather than simply speaking to them — what would the wounds prove that words alone might not?

Yawancinsu kuwa suna nan da rai, ko da yake waɗansu sun riga sun yi barci. <sup>7</sup>Sa’an nan ya bayyana ga Yaƙub, sa’an nan ga dukan manzannin, <sup>8</sup>a ƙarshe duka, ya bayyana a gare ni, kamar wanda an haifa a lokacin da bai kamata ba.

Ɗuɗɓe maɓɓe haa jooni e ɓe foofa, ammaa woɓɓe maayii. <sup>7</sup>7 Ɗonmaa o wanganii Yaakubu, nden fuu lilaaɓe maako. <sup>8</sup>8 Gaɗa maajum fuu, min maa, o wanganiiyam, koo nde e mi nandi e ɓingel danyaangel ko carel danyukingel waɗa. # KL 9.3‑6.
elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve. <sup>6</sup>Then he appeared to more than five hundred of the brothers and sisters at one time, most of whom are still alive, though some have fallen asleep. <sup>7</sup>Then he appeared to James, then to all the apostles. <sup>8</sup>Last of all, as though to one born at the wrong time, he appeared also to me.

elements.question3: Paul lists a range of witnesses to the risen Jesus. What is significant about the fact that he mentions over five hundred people at one time, ‘most of whom are still alive’?
elements.sampleAnswer: By noting that most of the five hundred are still alive, Paul is effectively issuing an open invitation to verify the claim. His readers could, in principle, go and ask these witnesses themselves. A fabricated story would not survive that kind of scrutiny — you do not cite five hundred living eyewitnesses to an event that did not happen. The sheer number also rules out the possibility of a private hallucination shared by just one or two people; a crowd of over five hundred experiencing the same thing simultaneously is a different category of evidence entirely.
elements.questionHint: Why might Paul have specifically pointed out that most of the five hundred are ’still alive’ — what would his original readers have been able to do with that information?

elements.text1: Wace nasara aka samu a ta dalilin mutuwa da tashin Yesu
elements.text3: What was achieved by the resurrection?

elements.passageText1:
<sup>54</sup># Ish 25.8  Sa'ad da kuma marar ruɓa ya maye mai ruɓar nan, marar mutuwa kuma ya maye mai mutuwar nan, a sa'an nan ne fa maganar nan da take a rubuce za a cika ta, cewa, <sup>55</sup>  # 
 Yush 13.14 
  “Ke mutuwa, ina nasararki? <sup>56</sup>Ai, zunubi shi ne ƙarin mutuwa, Shari'a kuwa ita ce sanadin ƙarfin zunubi.
elements.passageText3: <sup>54</sup>Sa’ad da mai ruɓan nan ya maye marar ruɓan nan, mai mutuwa kuma ya maye marar mutuwan nan, sa’an nan ne karin maganar da yake a rubuce zai zama gaskiya cewa, “An haɗiye mutuwa cikin nasara.” # 15.54  Ish 25.8 <sup>55</sup>“Ya ke mutuwa, ina nasararki? <sup>56</sup>Zunubi ne yake ba wa mutuwa dafi, Doka kuma take ba wa zunubi iko.
elements.passageText4: <sup>54</sup>54 Carel ngel ɓandu maayayndu nyolayndu wartiraa ɓandu ndu maayataa ndu nyolataa, ko aayaare wi'i ɗum heeway, waato, “Ɗum heɓii jaalorgal dow mayde!” # Ica 25.8. <sup>55</sup>55 “Mayde, to jaalorgal maaɗa? <sup>56</sup>56 Ngam hakke woni tooke pooɗayɗe mayde, nden bawɗe hakke to Attawra ƴiwi.
elements.passageText5: <sup>54</sup>Now when this perishable puts on the imperishable, and this mortal puts on immortality, then the saying that is written will happen: ‘Death has been swallowed up in victory.’ <sup>55</sup>’Where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?’ <sup>56</sup>The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law.

elements.question3: How does Paul describe what the resurrection has done to death? What is the ’sting’ of death, and why has it lost its power?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul describes death as having been ’swallowed up in victory’ — completely defeated and absorbed. He taunts death as though it were a beaten opponent: ‘Where is your victory? Where is your sting?’ The sting of death, Paul explains, is sin — sin is what gives death its lethal power, because sin incurs judgment. But Christ has dealt with sin on the cross. With sin dealt with and the resurrection accomplished, death has lost its sting. It can still happen physically, but it no longer has the final word or the power to condemn.
elements.questionHint: Paul tells us directly what the ’sting’ of death is — once you identify it, ask yourself what in this study has already dealt with that very thing.

elements.passageText1: <sup>11</sup># 1Kor 3.16  In kuwa Ruhun wannan da ya ta da Yesu daga matattu yana zaune a zuciyarku, to, shi da ya ta da Almasihu Yesu daga matattu zai kuma raya jikin nan naku mai mutuwa, ta wurin Ruhunsa da yake a zaune a zuciyarku.
elements.passageText3: <sup>11</sup>In kuwa Ruhu wannan da ya tā da Yesu daga matattu yana zama a cikinku, shi wanda ya tā da Kiristi daga matattu zai ba da rai ga jikunanku masu mutuwa ta wurin Ruhunsa, wanda yake zama a cikinku.
elements.passageText4: <sup>11</sup>11 To Ruuhu Allah umminɗo Yeesu daga mayde e jooɗoo nder ɓerɗe mooɗon, on ɗon umminɗo Almasiihu daga mayde hokkay ɓalli mooɗon maayayɗi yonki barka Ruuhu maako jooɗiiɗum nder ɓerɗe mooɗon. # 1Kor 3.16.
elements.passageText5: <sup>11</sup>Moreover if the Spirit of the one who raised Jesus from the dead lives in you, the one who raised Christ from the dead will also make your mortal bodies alive through his Spirit who lives in you.

elements.question3: What promise does Paul make to those in whom the Spirit lives? What connection does he draw between Jesus’ resurrection and ours?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul promises that the same God who raised Jesus from the dead will also make believers’ mortal bodies alive — through his Spirit who lives in them. The connection he draws is direct: it is the same power, the same agent (the Spirit), and the same God at work. Jesus’ resurrection is therefore not a one-off event for him alone; it is the pattern and guarantee of what awaits all who have the Spirit. What happened to Jesus will happen to his people.
elements.questionHint: Paul describes the Spirit using a phrase that points back to the resurrection of Jesus — how does that description link what happened to Jesus with what will happen to believers?

elements.text1: Menene tashin matattu yake koya mana game da Allah?
elements.text3: What does the resurrection teach us about God?

elements.passageText1: <sup>19</sup>da kuma ko mene ne girman ikonsa marar misaltuwa da yake zartarwa a gare mu, mu masu ba da gaskiya, wato zartarwar ƙarfin ikon nan nasa, <sup>20</sup># Zab 110.1  wanda ya zartar ga Almasihu sa'ad da ya tashe shi daga matattu, ya kuma ba shi wurin zama a damansa a samaniya,
elements.passageText3: <sup>19</sup>Ina addu’a ku san girman ikon Allah wanda ya wuce misali, wanda yake namu. Ikon da yake aiki a cikinmu, ɗaya ne da ikon nan <sup>20</sup>da ya tā da Kiristi daga matattu, ya kuma sa ya zauna a hannun damansa a cikin mulkin samaniya.
elements.passageText4: <sup>19</sup>19 Ɗonmaa, e mi waɗa do'aare andon no bawɗe maako mawɗe kuwirta nder meeɗen, enen tokkuɓe Yeesu. Bawɗe ɗen ɗe go'o e bawɗe cembiɗɗe <sup>20</sup>20 ɗe Allah huutiri carel ko o ummini Almasiihu daga mayde o jooɗinimo dow jooɗorgal laamu to jungo maako nyaamo ton dow. # Jab 110.1.
elements.passageText5: <sup>19</sup>and what is the incomparable greatness of his power toward us who believe, as displayed in the exercise of his immense strength. <sup>20</sup>This power he exercised in Christ when he raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand in the heavenly realms,

elements.question3: Paul says the resurrection displays God’s ’incomparable greatness’ of power. What does that mean for believers? How does knowing this affect the way you think about God?
elements.sampleAnswer: The word ’incomparable’ means there is nothing to measure it against — no greater power exists. And Paul says this same power is directed toward believers. The resurrection is therefore not just a demonstration of what God can do in general; it is a display of the power that is actively at work for those who believe. For how we think about God, this is significant: the God we are relating to is not limited, distant, or passive. He is the God who raised the dead, and that same strength is on our side.
elements.questionHint: Paul says this power is directed ’toward us who believe’ — so what does the resurrection tell us not just about God’s ability, but about his posture towards his people?

elements.text1: Tayaya Yesu tashi zuwa sama?
elements.text3: How did Jesus ascend into heaven?

elements.passageText1:
<sup>9</sup>  #

Yesun nan da aka ɗauke daga gare ku aka kai shi Sama, zai komo ne ta yadda kuka ga tafiyarsa zuwa Sama.”
elements.passageText3: <sup>9</sup>Bayan ya faɗi wannan, sai aka ɗauke shi sama a idanunsu sai wani girgije ya rufe shi daga ganinsu. <sup>10</sup>Suna zuba ido a bisa, zuwa cikin sararin sama yayinda yake tafiya, nan da nan, sai ga mutane biyu saye da fararen riguna tsaye kusa da su. <sup>11</sup>Sai suka ce, “Mutanen Galili, don me kuke tsaye a nan kuna duban sararin sama? Wannan Yesu, wanda aka ɗauke daga gare ku zuwa sama, zai komo daidai yadda kuka ga ya tafi sama.”

E ɓe ndaaramo haa duule cuddimo. # Mar 16.19; Luk 24.50,51. <sup>10</sup>10 E ɓe tijjii e ɓe ndaaramo e mo fa'i dow, sey raa worɓe ɗiɗo ɓe kolte daneeje mbangii haade maɓɓe e ndarii. <sup>11</sup>11 Worɓe ɓen mbi'iɓe, “Onon yimɓe Galiili, ngam ɗume ndariiɗon ɗo'o on ndaara dow? Yeesu on ɗon koccaaɗo nder mooɗon yaaki dow, o so''oyto non no ngiiruɗon o hocciraama dow.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>After he had said this, while they were watching, he was lifted up and a cloud hid him from their sight. <sup>10</sup>As they were staring into the sky while he was going, suddenly two men in white clothing stood near them <sup>11</sup>and said, ‘Men of Galilee, why do you stand here looking up into the sky? This same Jesus who has been taken up from you into heaven will come back in the same way you saw him go into heaven.’

elements.question3: How is the ascension described here? What promise accompanies Jesus’ departure, and what does that mean for the future?
elements.sampleAnswer: The ascension is described as a visible, witnessed event: the disciples watched as Jesus was lifted up and a cloud took him out of their sight. It was not a quiet disappearance but a deliberate, observable departure. The accompanying promise, delivered by two men in white, is that ’this same Jesus’ will come back in the same way he left. The phrase ’this same Jesus’ is important — not a different figure, but the very one they knew, who died and rose. The ascension therefore points forward: his departure is not the end but a guarantee of his return.
elements.questionHint: The messengers use the specific phrase ’this same Jesus’ — why might those three words be particularly important as a description of the one who will return?

elements.text1: Ina Yesu yake yanzu? Wanne iko yake da shi?
elements.text3: Where is Jesus now? What authority does He have?

Yana mulkin kome a wannan duniya, zai kuma yi mulkin duniya mai zuwa. <sup>22</sup>Allah ya sa kome a ƙarƙashin ikonsa, ya kuma ba shi iko a kan kome don amfanin ikkilisiya.
